column121: My FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying) and ATC kept us high for ILS 32 RIV.  After all attempts to go down and slow down; performing S turns and seeing the airport; knowing we could not possibly be stable by 1000 ft.; we elected to go around at about 1300 ft. AGL.When I told tower we were going around; she said 'yes; you're cleared to land'.  When I repeated that we were 'going around; we are too high to land'; she said 'oh; I misunderstood' and then proceeded to give us these instructions:  'Aircraft X; roger go around and at the end of the runway; turn left heading 140; climb and maintain 4000.My FO had initiated the go around before these instructions were given; and had started flying the MAP with an altitude of 5300 ft.; and as the PM (Pilot Monitoring) I was trying desperately to keep the airport in sight.  Even the field was reporting better than 'five and five'; there was fog everywhere else; and inflight visibility was about 3 miles in haze. We selected 4000 ft.; and verified it; but when my scan came back inside; we were at 4500 ft.  I instructed the PF to descend back down to 4000 ft. about the time tower said 'Aircraft X maintain 4000' and I responded that we were descending back down.When I changed back to approach control; we were level or about level and we were sequenced for a proper ILS and landed without incident.  We were not given a phone number; nor was there any known traffic conflict.I don't believe ATC has any idea what a go around is like.  We brief MAP procedures prior to our descent check; but we really don't know what ATC instructions will include on a go around because there are so many variables to consider.  I hate go arounds; but I hate writing ASAPs more.  A bit ironic because during cruise I was reading the latest Cleared Direct! Streamlining SOCAL approach into RIV would be helpful; 3 frequency changes in a few minutes is difficult; and they are so busy with GA it's easy to forget us.  By the time we checked on with Approach; he asked us to 'report established;' and when he cleared us for the ILS we were several thousand feet high.  I feel as the Captain; I should have been more focused inside; but our instructions preoccupied my scan outside for the go around.
column123: While on the ARKOE 1 Arrival ATC had us maintain 12;000 ft. until shortly after passing the ARKOE Fix. When handed off to Tower we were given the clearance to descend and maintain 4;000 ft. and subsequently cleared for the ILS Y 32 Approach. I was unable to descend in the allotted time to achieve the Stable Approach Criteria. I excited a Go Around. Tower was informed and was given vectors back to re-sequence for the approach. I then executed another approach and landed without incident.

column121: At normal 500 feet mark; Pilot Monitoring called 'Stable; Cleared to Land Runway XXL' to which Pilot Flying replied; 'Landing'.  Pilot Flying was observed to be within parameters of stable approach to land during all phases. Winds were variable out of the east. Pilot Monitoring remarked a CRJ holding short for departure on [Runway] XXL as the flight approached runway to land. As aircraft crossed threshold; aural cadence stated '50....'then; startling us both; it sounded '40; 30; 20; 10' rapidly as the cadence sped up. Pilot Flying held pitch and applied power to attempt to slow descent rate but it seemed as though the bottom dropped out immediately.  Aircraft landed firmly.It was a startling event and one which took us by complete surprise. I wonder if there could be a connection between the CRJ holding short; perpendicular to the numbers and the easterly; variable winds--possibly generating a unique mechanical turbulence which caused the rapid decay in descent so close to the runway.Everything leading up to the '50' aural call was stable and within normal parameters.  I'm not certain this event could have been 'prevented'; per se; as it happened so very quickly; and close to the runway.  Pilot Flying absolutely attempted to mitigate the rate by adding power and holding pitch.

column121: CA was Pilot Monitoring (PM) and FO was Pilot Flying (PF). As PF prior to descent into ZZZ [Airport]; reviewed ZZZZZ 4; NOTAMs; and ATIS for expected RNAV GPS Y XXL. ZZZ Center cleared descend via ZZZZZ 4. Handoff to ZZZ Approach modified ZZZZZ 4 descent to 6;000 feet instead of 4;000 feet. While enroute to terminating fix ZZZZZ2; redirected to ZZZZZ1 while maintaining 6;000 feet instead of lower altitude. Also; no type of approach was assigned yet. Please note ZZZZZ2 was not on approach plate RNAV Y GPS XXL. Since an unfamiliar identifier and location of ZZZZZ2 was given; utilized FMS waypoints for situational awareness. Discovered ZZZZZ2 was only 5 miles away while still at 6;000 feet instead of 3;100 feet. Proceeded LNAV direct ZZZZZ2 then subsequently cleared ILS XXL while still very high. Also; haze prevented additional situational awareness to closing distance to ZZZ. PM reset FMS approach; DA; NAVAIDs; and briefed approach. PF initiated descent; disconnected autopilot; and started landing configuration. However; very late type of approach assignment (different than ATIS expected approach approach) and unusually high altitude (6;000 feet instead of 3;100 feet) prior to GS intercept made it impossible to achieve full landing configuration by 1;000 feet. Initiated go-around and told to maintain runway heading to 3;000 feet then 5;000 feet. Cleaned up aircraft maintaining runway heading. During climb; ATC directed 120 heading climb to 5;000 feet. PM repeated 120 heading and altitude without turn direction. ATC did not correct read back with missing turn direction. Based on current aircraft heading; initiated shortest direction left hand turn to 120 heading. During turn; ATC asked for steeper turn and PF increased bank to 30 degrees. New vector given to 090 and further climb to 6;000 feet. Subsequently received vectors for ILS XXL. Nominal approach and landing ILS XXL. Normal gate arrival and shutdown.First; entire approach sequence must be considered in conjunction with the go-around event and subsequent climbout instructions. Second; this was the first flight after 4 months of leave for the PF and into an unfamiliar domestic destination. ZZZZZ 4 STAR was normal until approaching STAR terminating fix of ZZZZZ2. Third; directed to maintain higher than normal 6;000 feet instead of 4;000 feet. Fourth; upon reaching ZZZZZ2; approach directed to proceed to ZZZZZ1 without stating type of approach. Fifth; unfamiliar with newly assigned fix ZZZZZ1 which could not be found on ATIS expected approach of RNAV GPS Y XXL. Sixth; while at unusually high altitude; given approach clearance to ILS XXL. Seventh; higher than normal approach altitude (6;000 feet instead of 3;100 feet at ZZZZZ1) made it impossible to meet approach and landing configuration requirements. Eighth; during go-around; a possible partially blocked radio call from ATC provided only heading 120 and 3;000 feet. Ninth; PM provided read back of heading and altitude but ATC did not correct read back with missing turn direction. Tenth; based on current aircraft heading; turned shortest direction to assigned heading of 120. Eleventh; a partial turn instead of a 180 degree turn would help ensure proper turn direction. Twelfth; PF and PM were not aware of deviation until call to ATC. ATC should assign type of approach upon initial contact. ATC should avoid approach clearances different than ATIS expected approach clearances close to FAF. ATC should provide adequate time and distance for descents to better able air crews to become properly established on approach. Please note ATC required 6;000 feet instead of 3;100 feet approaching ZZZZZZ1 making it impossible to comply with approach and landing configuration requirements. Fourth; ATC should correct read backs of missing turn direction when required. Fifth; provide partial turns instead of 180 degrees to ensure proper turn direction.

column121: I had requested and was set up for the RNAV [approach]. At some point a few miles out from the initial fix the Controller informed me that the approach would intrude on a TFR and suggested the VOR [approach]. We had quite a lot of moderate turbulence and I was having difficulty loading the new approach; setting the minimums and; most importantly; briefing the approach to myself. I was cleared for the approach and to join the 10 DME arc which I believed I did. I was not getting a good VOR signal but GPS showed the inbound course approaching. As I turned inbound on the final course still trying to identify the VOR; the Controller asked why I was not on the arc. I started second-guessing my own procedure but was confident I would get the airport in sight as the AWOS I had been monitoring was reporting 2- 5 miles Vis over the last hour.At this point I looked over and realized I had mis-tuned the VOR freq. Due to the Controller's questioning; very bad turbulence; poor forward visibility and my admittedly botched approach; I decided to discontinue the approach. I was not confident of my position in relation to the missed approach point and elected; for safety; to climb straight ahead as I could see the mountains on either side and had good forward visibility above the smoke.I was then cleared for the RNAV (GPS) C approach and got the airport in sight and landed.I was given a phone number to call; which I did; and was advised of a possible pilot deviation.My interpretation of the clearance 'Join the 10 DME arc; cleared for the approach' was that I had been vectored to the approach. Later; upon review of the approach; I realized I should have flown to the IAF and joined the arc or simply requested the non-arc approach by flying to ZZZ and making the procedure turn.I allowed myself to get rushed in very poor flight conditions. I should have asked for vectors or holding in order to properly review the approach in the first place.

column121: Enroute from ZZZ to YKM last flight of the day.  Night conditions with smoke. Tower and Approach Control were closed. I was pilot flying.  Route was planned over V298 through DATVE intersection.  This route took us through a TFR due to fires northwest of Yakima.  Approaching DATVE; we got discretion to 11000 ft; and requested RNP RWY 9 starting at DATVE.  At first Seattle Center told us we may need to hold because of another airplane on another approach into Yakima; we told him we could do that; but he told us to do a right 360 and that we would be able to proceed inbound after that.  After making the turn we were told to track the approach inbound and were cleared down to 8500 ft; then 8100 ft.  Due to the TFR he couldn't clear us lower.  When he finally cleared us for the approach; we ended up really high but we elected to configure early and continue with the approach.  We had to use VS mode to descend because we could not get the VNAV linked being so high.  We protected the airplane with the altitude alerter and ALT SELECT for each segment.  When we cleared the smoke and could see the airport we were approaching YINUL and we noticed we were still quite high. At this point we were set up gear down and flaps 15 but I thought I had called for flaps 35.  We were not sure if we would be able to make it so we told Center we may need to come around for another approach.  A moment later we noticed the glidepath coming in so we elected to continue with a visual approach.  The controller re-cleared us for a visual and we switched to CTAF.  At some point we set the altitude alerter to field elevation and continued.  Thinking I was already at flaps 35 I called for below the line at the 1000 ft chime.  Right around the '500' call we got the aural warning 'TOO LOW - FLAPS' and I called for and executed a missed approach.  As we were climbing I elected to fly a visual left hand traffic pattern back to Runway 9 instead of the published missed approach procedure to avoid going back up into the smoke layer.  We set the alerter to 2600 ft and went around; but ended up climbing to about 4000 for extra safety margin.  We reactivated the approach for final approach guidance; turned base at about 3.5 miles and landed without incident on RWY 9. The flight from ZZZ to YKM is very quick.  Especially landing to the East.  I usually landed on 27 so was not as familiar with the approach to RWY 9 but we did note the high approach angle of 3.5 Deg.  And briefed it thoroughly.  The approach felt uncomfortably high; which led to us being distracted.  I was thinking about the potential missed approach and we missed the fact that the airplane was not fully configured for landing.  In retrospect we should have told ATC that we want to hold at DATVE until he could fully clear us for the approach once the other airplane landed. That would have given us enough time to get everything set and avoid distractions and time compression.  The other thing we could have done is requested vectors to the south and to start the approach from YKM VOR.  Or we could have set up for RWY 27 since the winds were almost a direct crosswind slightly favoring Runway 9 and under 10 kts.  The other thing I would do different next time at night is to fly the published missed approach procedure because of the terrain.  I felt comfortable with my knowledge of the terrain in Yakima; but flying the traffic pattern at night on the missed added another layer of risk that we didn't need at that point.

column121: This event was undoubtedly the most humbling and perhaps a first in my almost XX years with (airline) and going on X years as Captain.  While there will always be a first; it will certainly serve as a professional learning opportunity for me personally.  I take full accountability as Captain for not directing a go-around sooner.  The ZZZ1-ZZZ flight was leg three on a three day pairing.  We were approaching an 8 hour flight block and over 10 hour duty on our flight to ZZZ. Flight paperwork review prior to departure indicated weather conditions which ended up being inconsistent with what we encountered in ZZZ.  Due to an ATIS outage that had spanned several hours; we were only able to retrieve hourly METAR reports for ZZZ. The last hourly report indicated significant changes with respect to gusty crosswinds from the East with smoke slightly lowering visibility.  As it came close to time to brief; I sent a message to Dispatch inquiring about ZZZ ATIS.  The message we received back indicated there indeed was an outage.  Unfortunately we were out of range to obtain the VHF ATIS prior to our briefing; but later obtained ATIS information over VHF in our descent.  Prior to TOD; we briefed the approach that we anticipated in ZZZ; the visual backed up by the ILS for [Runway] XYR.  We discussed relevant threats of gusty crosswinds; night; fatigue and potential for arrival changes and runway re-assignment. I set up with the VSD to be able to monitor flight path. On initial hand off to ZZZ Approach; we were assigned Runway XYL.  As PM; I loaded  the approach for the ILS XYL in the FMC; set new baro minimums; entered the new runway data; and set up the NAV frequency/course for XYL; briefed the changes; and then ran the change triangle Descent checklist. Approach Control directed us to go direct ZZZZZ on the approach; maintain 4;000 until ZZZZZ; and were subsequently cleared for the approach. My FO as PF attempted to mitigate the threat of being high on path maintaining higher speed as directed previously by ATC (delete speed restrictions on the arrival) and I monitored using the VSD.As we joined final; it was evident from wind data on the PFD; that the winds on approach were strong challenging gusty crosswinds from the East.  After joining final for XYL; my FO struggled with maintaining the LOC course and GP with the gusty dynamic crosswinds.  With LOC and GP deviation call outs as PM; my FO did an outstanding job re-establishing proper path and we were able to achieve what we thought was stable criteria.  In the time frame of attempting to get better alignment established on final and simultaneously run the landing checklist; I inadvertently missed the final flap 30 selection; and we both missed the flap misconfiguration on the checklist.  While we ran the landing checklist; our attention was diverted enough to not note the flaps 25 configuration.  Cumulative fatigue from a long flying day with challenging weather contributed to the inadvertent omission.  It wasn't until the GPWS Flaps alert that we noted the misconfiguration.  Out of concern for speed due to gusty winds; I selected flaps 30 just as I directed the go-around due to unstable landing criteria.  While I normally look at flap configuration at the 500 feet gate as an additional check; the cumulative fatigue and dynamic wind conditions had my attention more focused on monitoring winds and flight path.  After having time to reflect on the encounter; the wisdom it has imparted is the absolute importance of an early go-around if conditions are not coming together as planned for an early stable approach. A go-around at 1;000; due to challenges with flight path would have afforded an easier go-around scenario with dynamic gusty winds after 8 hours of cumulative flying for the day.While we both had no currency issues and had sufficient recent experience; it's undeniable that pairing constructions that culminate in 8-9 hours of block time landing close to midnight on day threeof a trip that consisted of two to three legs every day with aircraft swaps only add to fatigue.  In this new era of COVID; there are added stresses which only serve to raise the overall underlying level of surge fatigue that exists from operating on the front lines and dealing with constant change.  While I take full accountability for my actions and omissions; I have concerns over the impact of future pairing construction given my recent encounters.With respect to lessons learned; our encounter highlights the importance of an early go-around in dynamic weather conditions when the approach is not coming together as planned.  As mentioned in the narrative; it's undeniable that pairing constructions that culminate in 8-9 hours of block time landing close to midnight on day three of a trip that consisted of two to three legs every day with aircraft swaps only add to cumulative fatigue.  We are operating in a new normal; and pairing design and construction should consider the added underlying level of fatigue and operational stress that cannot be easily mitigated.  Pairings with a 3-2-3 leg-day design with multiple aircraft swaps should be strongly avoided so as to not be conducive to creating conditions ripe for encounters such as the one described above.

column121: I was on an IFR Flight Plan from ZZZ to ZZZ1.  I had poor speed control on the approach.  I was legally IFR current; but; as it turned out; I was clearly not proficient.  The COVID-19 prevented me from flying with a (COVID safe) safety pilot and getting recent practice.  I was scheduled for simulator training for the following week.I flew VFR out to ZZZ for two days.  The weather was predicted VMC for the next several days.  The day after we arrived; the wind shifted and blew in severe smoke from forest fires.  We considered staying for a few more days; but there was no available lodging and the smoke was getting worse.  I reviewed an IFR plan to go back to ZZZ1 and when the destination weather improved enough; I filed IFR; got my clearance; and departed.  The departure; on route; and vectors to final were smooth and uneventful. There was an aircraft ahead that was going slower and ATC requested that I slow to 90 knots; which is what I should have already been slowing to.  I agreed and reduced power.  The autopilot was holding the altitude coming up to the IAF and I inadvertently pulled off too much power.  I was coming up on the IAF and descent and I was verifying that everything (else) was ready to go; when I noticed that the speed was rapidly reducing.  The autopilot (AP) was trimming nose up to maintain altitude.  I hit control wheel steering; nosed down; and added power.  I had to do a lot of trimming to get the nose back down and I over shot the altitude and rapidly picked up speed.  I got the nose back up; on altitude; and thought that the AP would acquire the glide slope.  Nope; in the excitement; I missed arming the approach and flew right through it.  Now I dove for the GS and picked up more speed again.  Now I'm on the GS and check to see if the AP would acquire it and no it started another excursion and so I shut it off and hand flew the rest of the way.  It was stabilized at this point; so I continued the approach and landed.Mistake 1.  I should have already been planning and reducing speed approaching IAF.Mistake 2.  I should have paid more attention to my speed reduction technique.Mistake 3.  I should have requested ATC to bring me around again to have time to set up properly.Mistake 4.  I should have done what I normally do and that is to turn off the AP and hand fly anytime things get weird.My takeaway is that my IFR currency should be done more often; even if it is done in a simulator.

column121: During an ILS approach to Runway XXR; the PF failed to slow early enough on final approach. PF configured later than desired considering the situation (fast due to earlier ATC request) resulting in the aircraft becoming high on glide path while slowing to configure for landing. PF needed to use a sink rate higher than desired to return to glide slope. We met the published parameters at all of our approach gates; but the first portion of the approach was unstable. We landed safely on XXR and proceeded routinely to the gate.The main lesson learned was to slow earlier on final approach to allow a more stable approach. We accomplished all checklists and procedures and landed safely; but the crew would have been more able to look for potential threats if the PF had flown a more stable approach during the first portion of the approach. PNF was just back from several months off - take away for PNF was to address a rapidly evolving situation sooner and prompt the PF to configure sooner.

column121: While on RNAV RWY 4 approach into UIN; between the initial fix (JISNA) and the FAF (WUSIS); we descended below the minimum altitude prescribed for that segment of the approach; which was 2100 ft. MSL . I did not recognize that we were below until about 1600 ft. MSL and by the time I had corrected our pitch and added power we between 1500 ft. and 1600 ft. MSL. We immediately climbed back up to 2100'; and at about 2 miles prior to the FAF; we re-evaluated our position; flight guidance panel and configuration and felt that we could resume the approach as normal; which was an autopilot coupled LPV approach. Part of the cause of this issue was my set up of the flight guidance panel. We had been descending from about 7000 ft. and navigating direct to the initial fix of JISNA. At some point in the descent; ATC cleared us for the approach and told us to maintain 2700 ft. until JISNA. We were given the frequency change from Center to CTAF at this point as well. At that point; I thought I was in VNAV mode and descending. I hit APPR (Approach); since we were cleared for the approach; and dialed my altitude selector to the bottom of the approach; which was 960 ft. MSL for the minimums. My assumption was that we would then VNAV to 2700 ft. at JISNA; or essentially just VNAV to 2100 ft. by WUSIS; grab the glideslope and continue the descent. I felt I had set it up so that all stepdown altitudes would be met. At that point; I may have become distracted with other radio calls and prepping for landing. During this time; the SIC (Second In Command) was running checklists; trying to reach the FBO  on unicom; and also conferring with ATC to ensure we had a good way to cancel IFR  once we were on the ground. Neither of us were totally focused on the task at hand; which was to monitor the plane as we were beginning this approach. I estimate we were doing about an 800 fpm descent rate. We may have been in VNAV mode; but I was not coupled to the glide path; so we were descending in such a way that would not allow the autopilot to level us off at each fix. 1. PIC/SIC should have been totally focused on the primary task at hand: entering; establishing; and flying the approach as published. This would have caught any errors in my programming of the descent and flight guidance panel before we descended below published altitude. 2. Both pilots should be looking at the flight guidance panel in these critical times to verify proper mode; and calling out altitudes around these fixes. 3. Once the altitude deviation was realized; I should have climbed up; executed a missed approach; and called ATC to vector us back around for the approach. 4. My decision not to go missed was influenced by my desire to keep my passenger's schedule. That is a hazardous attitude that I want others to be aware of and learn from.
column123: As our passengers were boarding the plane; they decided to change the destination to UIN due to the fact we were unlikely to be able to get into ZZZ due to low ceilings. The altitude deviation occurred on the RNAV (Area Navigation) approach to RW 4 at UIN. We had been cleared for the approach and we were to instructed to maintain 2700 ft. until established on the approach and had been approved to change to advisory frequency. I was the nonflying pilot and was busy verifying everything on the checklist had been completed and communicating with KC center and UNICOM at UIN to make position reports. I also verified with KC center that we would be able to contact them on the ground using that frequency or did we need to cancel our IFR through flight service. This took a little extra time to complete due to my lack of experience with the Proline Fusion avionics so I was not monitoring the PFD as closely as I should have. We were inside JISNA (IAF)and I was trying to determine our position from the airport in order to give an accurate position report when the pilot flying said something to the effect of wait why are we low. He immediately added power selected vertical speed up and set our altitude selector to 2100 ft. I was focused on other tasks in the cockpit so I cannot say with 100% certainty what autopilot modes were selected previously. We were somewhere between 1600 ft. and 1500 ft. MSL when he started the recovery. We reached 2100 ft. two miles prior to the WUSIS (FAF) intercepted the glide path and landed without further issue. During the debrief with my captain after the flight we discussed the need to treat such a critical phase of flight with the utmost respect and after getting established on the approach to keep all attention on the task at hand which is flying/monitoring the approach. We also discussed the need to make a verbal call out of all autopilot/ flight director mode changes to make sure both pilots are on the same page and are totally aware of what the automation is doing if it is engaged.

column121: On approach into ZZZ on ILS XR we had been asked to keep our speed up until the marker. We complied with the request. The weather was very gusty winds and both myself and the pilot flying communicated as to what the V-ref target speeds should be. Sometime before 1000 feet the pilot flying was trying to control his speed and configure at the last minute in order to comply with the Tower's request. We had traffic about 3 1/2 miles in front of us. With the gusty winds and both of us pilots being relatively new to the aircraft and because this aircraft was an X type aircraft I had relayed to the pilot flying that we didn't want to have a tail strike with such gusty winds; so I suggested that at any time either one of us felt that we weren't going to land in the touchdown zone or we didn't feel we weren't going to make a normal landing then we needed to 'go-around.' About 1200 feet we got another gusty wind report from the Tower that caused me to readjust the target speed with my head being down and losing situational awareness (SA). We never got too fast for our configuration but I called the'1000 feet' call at approximately 930 feet. I had already configured the aircraft for the landing checklist except for final flaps. I feel the pilot flying got distracted by me changing the target speeds that he lost his SA and forgot to call for flaps 30/landing checklist. I initiated the checklist and realized we were hadn't gone to flaps 30 and he hadn't called for the checklist. I selected final flaps and finished the checklist. I called 500 feet and he responded stable and we landed uneventfully.
column123: On arrival into ZZZ I was configured flaps 15; 180 knots until the marker. About the time I was going to call flaps 30 we received a new gusting  wind report from tower that required an adjustment to our target speed. We discussed the wind and adjusted for about a 15 knot add over flaps 30 ref of about 148 target speed. Upon completing landing checklist which at this point was also delayed and we discovered that the flaps were not positioned yet to 30. We immediately positioned the flaps to 30 and landed. The flaps 15 speed I was flying was very close to the new target speed and the aircraft was stable with respect to airspeed altitude and course. Contributing factors can include my new position as a requalification on the aircraft with only about 4 landings this year.

column121: I was the First Officer and PF ZZZ1-ZZZ. This was my first flight after not flying for over two months. The majority of the flight went fine; but as we approached ZZZ; ATC left us high (11;000 feet) very close to the airport. I was about to ask for lower when ATC gave us a descent. I thought we had just enough time to successfully descend and slow down; but when we were around 4;000 feet; ATC gave us a turn that made it clear they were directing us straight to the FAF. This was not what I was expecting; and left us high and fast and greatly increased our workload. I used the flight spoilers to slow down and then forgot to stow them after slowing enough to add flaps. Our workload was still quite high at this point; so we didn't realize the flight spoilers were still deployed until the spoiler caution message at 500 feet. At this point; the Captain immediately stowed them and we continued the landing.Because ATC left us high and then turned us directly to the FAF; our workload greatly increased. I deployed the spoilers in order to slow down; and then forgot to stow them because I was distracted by other things in the flight deck (arming the approach; making sure we intercepted the localizer and glideslope; making sure we were on speed and deployed our flaps and gear at the appropriate time; etc.). I was expecting something different from ATC and had a good plan in my head; but was thrown off when ATC gave us something I didn't expect. This was also my first leg after not flying for over 2 months; which I think could have contributed as well; because I was a bit rusty and a little bit slower than I normally am in the flight deck.We could have easily prevented this from happening by asking ATC for a longer downwind. I was planning a longer downwind in my head and when ATC gave us a turn directly to the FAF; we should have asked to stay on downwind instead. There wasn't anyone else out there to cause traffic issues; and it would have given us extra time to slow down and descend so that we weren't rushed. In the heat of the moment; I didn't think of this (though I used to remind my students of this option all the time when I was a flight instructor!); but I should have. When we stowed the spoilers; I decreased thrust and pitched down a little bit; which kept us on speed and caused only a slight deviation from the glideslope; which I was able to correct. Because of this; we made a completely normal landing. However; a go-around would also have been a good choice here; especially if we became unstable after retracting the spoilers.

column121: 3rd day of flying. 3rd flight of day. We had set up for the ILS Approach in accordance with ATIS. No mention was made of Visual Approaches being in use. Despite that fact; we briefed the restrictions over a satellite airport as a precaution.We were taken off the RNAV Arrival and were given a series of step down clearances as we approached the field. Simultaneously; we were told to expect the RNAV Visual Approach. The Pilot Monitoring loaded the new approach as we were below 10;000 ft. which I as the Pilot Flying rapidly briefed. Shortly thereafter; we were cleared to a point not on the arrival nor the RNAV Visual. As the Pilot Monitoring began searching for the fix; I maintained a 3:1 descent rate via VSpeed/Open Des. I was also approaching the point where I was going to need to configure if I wished to have a stabilized approach. The Pilot Monitoring found the fix on the RNAV (GPS) Approach and raced to reconfigure the FMS. Then; having given no prior warning; the Approach Controller cleared us for the ILS Approach which would have been impossible at this point. Having the runway in sight and fearing that any further 'assistance' from the controller would do little to aid my rapidly deteriorating situational awareness; I elected to disconnect the autopilot; and fly the approach visually. I pulled my Pilot Monitoring back into the loop by asking for him to clear my flight directors; configure the aircraft; and ask approach for the Visual Approach. The controller cleared us for the Visual Approach; 'as long as we fly the glideslope down'; which I'd submit is not a legal clearance; but was in any case irrelevant as following the glideslope had always been our intention. After doing some fishing to configure the aircraft and get down onto glideslope; the Pilot Monitoring reselected the approach and flight directors and we flew a stabilized approach to landing. In debriefing this event; we discovered that the fix was also present on the ILS Approach; which was the approach that ATIS had specified; the approach that we had initially briefed; and presumably the approach that the controller wished us to fly all along. I can't say what the Approach controller was thinking during this event. However; on an approach as infamous as any to I can only suggest that last minute changes are inappropriate and unsafe. I also think that the controller has an inaccurate impression of what is required of aircrews when changing approaches. I would suggest that the reality of flying this approach be made clear to her as controlling that is this cavalier will only lead to further unstabilized approaches in the future; or worse. The last minute change to the RNAV Visual followed by clearing us to a point not on the approach; followed by a second clearance for the ILS risked completely overwhelming the aircrew. That this was on a runway that is notorious for unstabilized approaches only exacerbated matters. Frankly; any acceptance of other than ILS approaches to this runway should be disallowed. Flying Visual Approaches to this runway; with it's unique peculiarities is a procedure designed by ATC; for the benefit of ATC. By definition and data; flying this Visual Approach is a compromise of safety for the sake of expediency; and sets aircrew up to be 'the last line of defense'; with generally predictable results.

column121: While executing a visual approach to Runway XX ZZZ airport; I was on a proper visual glide path until approximately 1/2 - 1/4 mile final.  A breakdown in my scan resulted in descending below a normal glide path; as a result we were unstable below 500 feet AGL.  Neither pilot called for a go-around; though; after debrief it was agreed that a go-around would have been appropriate. I was able to correct back on profile; approximately at 1/4 mile short final. The landing was safe; on speed and uneventful.Contributing factors included light turbulence requiring constant power adjustments. Also; lack of recent flight time due to taking leave--this was my first approach/landing in a number of weeks on top of very limited flight time in the past 6 months. Additionally; as experienced from the previous ILS approach to the same runway conducted earlier the same day; it was noticed that while on or above glide path; the PAPI's indicated well below; despite glide slope and VGSI not being coincident per the procedure.  The note on the procedure indicated that the VGSI would bring the aircraft across the threshold at approximately 50 feet; however; coming across the threshold at 80 feet; the PAPI's still indicated low (one white over three red).  This issue was briefed to be mitigated by referencing altitude/distance check points on descent; however; it still became an issue.  The lack of proper visual guidance may have contributed to a departure from a stable glide path; however; did not contribute to the failure to call a go-around.  The PAPI guidance issue was brought to the attention of the airport manager.The potential lack of flight time/currency due to COVID is a potential universal issue.  I have a luxury of flying with the X Group to maintain a scan but for those that don't; a severely reduced schedule will likely result in some loss of proficiency.  A solution may be to promote those with reduced flying to take more legs/landings than normal within a given pairing.  Additionally; aside from notifying the airport or ATC of malfunctioning equipment; e.g.; PAPI's; there should be a means by which to relay this information to the company for immediate communication to air crew; as there is no guarantee of a swift response or fix.  (aside from NOTAMs).
column123: Was pilot monitoring. We were coming in to land on the visual to Runway XX in ZZZ. During the last 1/4 to 1/2 mile on the approach. The pilot flying misjudged the distance and descent rate during the approach and started to get low on the approach. Pilot flying corrected but we were unstabilized on the approach and did not execute a go-around. We landed from the approach without incident within the touchdown zone area on the runway. The pilot flying had not been flying very much recently so lack of recent experience was a factor. A failure of the pilot monitoring to call for a go-around from an unstable approach. A failure of the pilot flying to call for a go-around from an unstabilized approach. Complacency on approach and not wanting to inconvenience passengers. Poor decision making by pilots.As pilots we have to always remember safety is top priority. Even if it's an inconvenience to both [of] you and everyone on board. If an approach becomes unstabalized a go-around or missed approach must be executed. Even if the landing happens safely and the landing is 'salvaged' it's important to remember that one day it might not be.  As a pilot monitoring if you see an unstabilized approach you should speak up. Don't worry about hurt feelings. Missed connections. We can't get complacent and think that we can just continue from an unstabilized approach.

column121: Throughout the arrival ATC requested best forward speed; comply at ZZZZZ: 230kts; 10;000 ft. After ZZZZZ; we were vectored for the approach to XXL. We called the airport in sight and ATC cleared us for the approach. We were also requested to slow to 160kts for spacing with an aircraft in front of us for the same runway. At this point I had flaps 8; and requested flaps 20. [Another] flight was also being vectored for XXL; coming from the west. ATC then asked us to slow to final approach speed and if we had the aircraft ahead insight. We did not have it insight; so were quickly told to turn left to a heading of 270. I believe the [other] flight was then cleared for the approach ahead of us; behind the first aircraft we were following. They then gave us a right turn back to the East and told us to intercept the localizer for XXL; but we were 90 degrees to the course and wouldn't have been able to make it and we weren't configured. I think I remember the controller stating that 'this isn't going to work' and asked if we could accept runway XXR. We said yes; and ATC began vectoring us for XXR. At this point I was very task saturated and should've done a go around. I was very concerned that the aircraft was set up for XXR and verbalized this. He said it was. In hindsight; I should have just disconnected the autopilot. We were configured by the FAF and a stable approach was completed. A go around should have been executed before this situation got so far. We did not have time to find the original aircraft in front of us; and then the [other] flight was thrown into the situation. When ATC switched us to XXR; a localizer frequency should have been offered. The Captain called the tower after we landed to discuss what happened with the supervisor. He said they were happy he called; and the supervisor was watching the whole situation. He also agreed a report should be completed.
column123: After being vectored on to final for runway XXL from the west at on approach control frequency; I called the airport in sight after we descended below the clouds; approximately 3;000 ft. AGL; ATC cleared us for the visual approach and instructed a speed reduction to 160 KIAS. There was another aircraft on final about 4 miles ahead of us. I offered to slow to approach speed and the controller immediately requested for us to do so while asking if we could also see the aircraft ahead of us. I indicated that I was unable to see an aircraft ahead. First Officer already had flaps 8 and called for flaps 20 to start slowing when I replied that I was unable to see the other aircraft. Suddenly ATC issued instructions for us to turn left to a heading of 270. At the same time; [another] flight was cleared to join the localizer ahead from the opposite direction; from the east; and midway between us and the previous aircraft. As we rolled out on the 270 heading; we were instructed to turn back to the right to a heading of 090 to intercept the localizer. As we rolled through 360 I could clearly see the [other] aircraft; rolling onto final about 2 miles ahead of us. By then it appeared as if the controller was going to want us to join the LOC on almost a 90 degree angle; not fully configured on a 3 mile final. I was about to ask for re-sequence when the controller asked if I could take runway XXR. I responded that we could; without taking the time to consult the flying pilot. As we crossed the approach path for XXL I was quickly pulling up the LOC frequency for XXR and trying to get the frequencies retuned. [Pilot Flying] asked if I was sure it was XXR and not XXC. I followed procedure and got verification from ATC prior to tuning it on the RTU (Radio Tuning Unit). This seemed to take longer than [Pilot Flying] had expected and I was not yet fully aware of his level of stress over the sudden unnecessary changes. We were then vectored through the XXR LOC and given right turns back towards the airport and cleared for a visual to XXR. The approach and landing was stabilized and without further incident. In retrospect; I think not giving [Pilot Flying] time to give his input about accepting a different runway; understandably added to his stress. While I may not have been uncomfortable with what I saw outside the window; I could have made my fellow pilot less comfortable by not asking him what he was thinking. Other contributory factors were compounded when the controller did not offer a LOC frequency whilst changing runways after getting us to less than optimal airspeed and giving us an impromptu tour of his airspace. Our company procedures require us to use an ILS as a backup whenever available; even in visual conditions. This meant that our crew had to simultaneously deal with clarification of communications; following ATC instructions; practice slow flight; and setting up navigation for a late runway change. To add to the equation; there were 80 souls in the main cabin who were certainly wondering why we were zig-zagging around the airport. I think most pilots don't like being loaded up that much in the simulator. Even less do we enjoy such practice in a real airplane at low altitude; and in the most critical phase of flight. Once at the gate; I asked the tower for a phone number to Approach and was given the number that connected me immediately to the supervisor who had been standing behind the controller during the entire ordeal. ATC stated first that he was glad I called. He then explained to me that he had told the controller to re-sequence us and not try to save the situation after his first mistake had put us in a position for an unstable approach. He apologized for the inconvenience and indicated that the [other] aircraft should not have been squeezed into the line. He should have sent the [other] aircraft to runway XXR to begin with. But by the time the situation had developed; it was better for us to break off of the approach than toattempt to turn the other aircraft around. [The supervisor] was professional and wanted to reassure me that such traffic management is not acceptable and that the controller at the helm during the situation was made well aware of that fact. He and I agreed that a report should be filed if it has the potential prevent at least some similar situations from occurring. I can't speak for the ATC practices and procedures; but from my perspective; there are a few variables at play here that should receive a little more focus in the near future. This applies several large class B airports facilities as well warrants adequate and candid discussion. I can't speak for the ATC practices and procedures; but from my perspective; there are a few variables at play here that should receive a little more focus in the near future. This applies several large class B airports facilities as well warrants adequate and candid discussion. RNAV arrivals have been created to help reduce controller and pilot work load as well as simplify clearance delivery. Issue them; let us all fly them as published; line us up; and watch us keep the line moving as you switch us to the next sector. Trust us to do our part as pilots. TCAS tells us how far behind the next aircraft we are. You want separation; ask us to keep 4 to 5 miles and we will do it. We will be far less confused and take up less time on frequency getting clarification of what you actually need us to do. Keeping the frequency more clear will also allow for us confused pilots to actually ask for the help we need when we don't understand something rather than have to wait for a lengthy exchange while a potentially adverse situation gets worse. It would also be nice if there were some regularly scheduled meetings between professional pilot groups and ATC groups. Before 9/11; it was as easy as asking on the radio if we could come visit the tower in between flights. But after all the bureaucrats got involved; common sense seemed to get tossed right out the window and now we have a generation of pilots who have never been to an ATC facility; seen a radar screen; or had the chance to ask questions directly to a controller and vice versa! I would love to see that decision get more attention.

column118: Flight Crew Became Reoriented
column121: I was conducting a visual approach to [Runway] XXR backed up by the RNAV Y XXR. I was the Pilot Flying and the Captain was the Pilot Monitoring. We had briefed all the threats and the approach per the AOM prior to the TOD and were planning flaps full for the landing configuration. We were being vectored onto a base leg to join the final visually. I was faster than I should have been on the base for the distance we were out (180 selected speed I think) and so I had to delay calling for the flaps. I think the last altitude we were given was 8;000 feet on the base before we reported the field in sight and were cleared for the visual. The approach had been activated and I went to managed speed. We then set a lower altitude just above the altitude for the next fix after the one just ahead of us on the approach so I could use vertical speed to start down and stay on the path since the approach was not quite captured. I had armed the approach and it annunciated FINAL APP as we were joining the final and the aircraft continued descending on the path. I was using the auto pilot for lateral and vertical NAV until established on final. We extended the gear prior to joining the final to help with slowing the aircraft to flap deployment speeds. The vector had put us in closer than I had anticipated and I had also started slowing later than I should have. The speed did not come back very quickly; possibly because of a tailwind shifting to a headwind as were turning final; so I had to disconnect the autopilot and arrest the descent to get the aircraft slowed for flap deployment and also to prevent an overspeed. Both the Captain and I noticed the RA was close to 1;000 [feet] as we deployed final flaps; and our speed was still well above approach speed; but we were VMC and our assessment was that we had been in final configuration prior to 1;000 AGL. We continued the approach and were stable at 500 [feet] so we landed out of the approach within the required criteria. We were at the gate when the ACARS printed a Self-Assessment Flight Report. We weren't surprised to see Y for speed and descent; but we were surprised to see a Y for flap change. We discussed it; and although we were unstable at 1;000 [feet]; we felt that we had been configured and thought the report generated a flap change Y at 0974 RA because the terrain rises to the north and the spot elevations are around 300-600 feet higher than the airport elevation near where we selected final configuration. Regardless; the final configuration selection was closer to 1;000 AGL than it should have been and we decided we would file an report just in case. Normally; I call for gear down and managed speed at 2;000 AGL so as to have plenty of time to reach final configuration prior to 1;000 AGL; but I missed that cue because I was in the yellow due to being tighter and faster than I had anticipated; and I didn't realize it because I was busy trying to get back in the green slowing and turning final and descending to stay on the path. This is also a case where my experience exceeded my performance due to being new to being back in the right seat and not flying much in September. I also didn't say anything about being in the yellow; and so I didn't get as much help as I could have used. I also failed to brief that we may need to be configured on base depending on where the base leg is and to say something if I don't ask for the gear down by 2;000 AGL. The other factor was conducting a visual approach to a high-altitude airport where AGL altitudes happen at much higher MSL altitudes than were are used to and neither I nor the Captain had been into ZZZ in a long time. I will more thoroughly brief visual approaches regarding the gates I want for configuration and speed changes that provide plenty of margin for a stabilized approach.
column123: As we turned to intercept final and configure for landing with a near 0 VSI; a hill on final gave us an unexpected 1;000 ft. radio alt call. We where visual and comfortable with continuing the approach due to distance. I recall being fully configuration when the second 1;000 foot reading on final and on speed at 500 ft. We failed to slow down in time on base leg not taking into effect high alt difference and ground speed. We did not brief the ground speed difference and the delayed slow down or terrain around the base turn. Haven't been to ZZZ in years.

column121: While being vectored by Approach Control on approach to Runway XXC at ZZZ; we were held at 4;000 ft. on the base leg with instructions to maintain 210 KIAS. We were subsequently given clearance for a Visual Approach to Runway XXC. The pilot flying from the left seat was the PIC (Pilot in Command). I was the SIC (Second in Command)/pilot monitoring from the right seat and acknowledged the visual approach clearance. The pilot flying made appropriate use of power levers; speed breaks; flaps; and landing gear to set up for a stabilized approach as he attempted to descend and intercept a normal glide path on final approach to the runway; while maintaining the requested 210 knot speed restriction. Once established on final; but still above a normal glide path; the PIC asked me to query ATC as to whether we could resume normal approach speed. The controller replied; 'When I clear you for a visual approach; the speed restriction is removed.' Noting that we were still above the PAPI and glidepath on final approach as a result of being kept at high altitude on the base leg and while descending through approximately 2;000 ft.; both crewmembers agreed that the aircraft could likely be configured to salvage a stabilized approach. However; as we descended through approximately 1;000 ft.; we realized that we had not been handed off to the control tower. As the approach control frequency was busy; I attempted to switch to the control tower frequency automatically; and was trying to reference the approach chart on the pilot's side display for the appropriate frequency; as there were three listed for the various runways. As a break in communications presented itself; I quickly queried approach control; 'Aircraft X to tower?' The controller responded that we should already be on the tower frequency; which he had not yet instructed us to do; and which was not the published frequency for Runway XXC. As the aircraft was now on short final; slightly above glide path; notwithstanding the long length of the runway; and the fact that we had not received landing clearance from the tower; the crew immediately agreed to go around; and smoothly initiated a go around procedure. I checked in with ZZZ Tower to advise we were going around. We were vectored by tower in a left hand traffic pattern at an appropriate altitude; without speed restriction; and were able to properly configure; execute a visual approach and land without incident.The crew could have solicited a lower altitude from Approach on the base leg and/or stated that we were unable to maintain the 210 KIAS speed restriction in order to establish ourselves on the final approach course at an appropriate altitude and speed for a stabilized approach. We could also have solicited a handoff from approach to tower frequency sooner. The decision to go around was prudently made and smoothly executed. However; we should have made the decision to do so at 1;000 feet AGL due to our position above the normal glide path (unstable approach) and not having been handed off to tower.
column123: While being vectored for a visual approach to XXC at ZZZ; approach had us turning a close in base at 4;000 ft. and 210 knots as assigned. Once we were on the final approach course; we were cleared for the visual approach. I knew that we were both too high and too fast; however I thought with the use of drag devices we could become stable prior to 1000 ft. per our FOM. I noticed that we were on a 2-3 mile final and had not been switched to the tower controller yet and asked the PNF (Pilot Not Flying) to query the approach controller. The approach controller stated that he had handed us over to the tower controller; neither of us in the cockpit heard that or acknowledged that instruction. The PNF was searching on the approach chart for a tower frequency and there was some confusion as he was trying the read the chart and which frequency he should be on. By the time I could have the PNF query the controller again; I realized that we were not going to be stable within the parameters of the FOM. I made the decision to go-around and informed the PNF to tell the approach controller we were executing a missed approach. The approach controller gave us instructions to fly runway heading to 3000 ft. and vectored us around for another visual approach which ended up in a stable; uneventful landing.I should have realized earlier on in the approach that we were vectored onto the final approach course too fast and too high. The PNF should have preloaded the tower frequency into the Radio Tune page. The decision to execute a go-around was correct but could have possibly been made sooner.

column121: Upon final approach to XXL on [an] ASR approach into ZZZZ the Pilot Flying elected and performed a go around approximately 2-3 miles from runway. The decision to go around was due to being high on glide path and in bad position to land at the final descent point. After the go around; Approach Control gave vectors to 7-mile final for another approach to XXL. The Pilot Flying was able to acquire a better sight picture and glide path; then landed without further issue. The crew had to do a radar approach as no approaches for ZZZZ are available in the FMC database for the open runway; XXL/XR. The ASR final descent point did not put the aircraft in a position for pilot flying to see PAPIs to refine glide path. In addition; the sloping terrain just off the end of the runway gives illusion of lower position on glide slope that actual position. Runway XXR/XL is closed for an extended duration due to construction. All approaches in FMC database are for the closed runway. Our company version of Flight Deck Pro has approaches available to XXL/XR so consideration should be given to adding these approaches to the FMC database since ZZZZ is [an] airport with regular flights by company aircraft.

column121: On base leg to Runway XXL at ZZZ called runway in sight and cleared for visual approach.  Aircraft was on a 90 degree intercept to runway and PF was delaying turn towards airport.  2;000 feet was selected on FCU and descent continued for approach.  Pilot Monitoring offered and executed direct to FAF to help with FMS sequencing and provide later guidance towards airport. After completing FMS programming 1;000 foot auto callout sounded.  Since aircraft was configured gear down and flaps 3 we continued with this configuration.  Since aircraft was low on profile we reduced descent to level off and intercept glide path from below.First trip for Pilot Flying in 2 weeks.  First trip back for Pilot Monitoring in 5 weeks.  End of 8:30 minute flying day.Better situational awareness concerning altitude on visual approach at night.  Less heads down time for programming FMS during night visual approach.

column121: On ILS [Runway] XXL configured for an autoland upon hearing GPWS warning a go-around was initiated by me and successfully vectored back around for another approach to [Runway] XXR that was successful. In the debrief we both recognized the aircraft was not fully configured for landing thus the warning. Mitigating factors length of duty day; complacency due to the fact the approach was going well; maybe stress due to the current events i.e.; COVID-19; and company uncertainty in today's world. Threats also included new Captain IOE and check airman flying in right seat.  We all have been flying less.
column123: I was flying as the LCA and pilot monitoring.  While conducting new Captain IOE on Aircraft X; we were setting up for an autoland on Runway XXL in ZZZ.  The arrival and approach were briefed to include known and anticipated threats.  We were vectored around for the approach and cleared for the ILS XXL.  The approach mode was armed and the second autopilot engaged.  Just prior to intercepting the glideslope; the gear was lowered and flaps 5 was selected.  We were in VMC conditions with the runway in sight.  The touchdown zone altitude was set as we continued the approach.  The 1;000 feet call and 500 feet call were made by me acting as pilot monitoring.  The response to 'set missed approach altitude cleared to land' and 'flare armed stable' were made respectively by the IOE student.  After 500 feet we received GPWS alerts for 'Terrain' and as we approached the runway threshold; a 'Pull Up' warning was [received].  After observing that we were in normal landing position; I made the ill advised call to continue.  As the autopilot began to enter the flare we realized we were still at flaps 5 and not configured to land.  I immediately called for a Go-Around and the Go-Around procedure was executed.  We advised ATC and were vectored around for a second approach for [Runway] XXR.  We executed the approach and landed without further incident.  Upon arriving at the gate; we debriefed our errors as well as the breakdown in CRM/TEM monitor crosscheck and SOP.  We also debriefed our failure to adhere to the GPWS warning.  We concluded that the combination of limited currency; outside current distractions (COVID; displacements;) and a long duty day may have created a moment where we completely lost focus.  The decision to go-around saved our poor performance from becoming an accident.

column121: VMC approach to Runway XXR at ZZZ at night; PAPI OTS. We were cleared the visual on a right base to the FAF. We flew the approach in LNAV/VNAV and showed on glidepath as we approached the turn to final. I had gear down; flaps 15 as we rolled out and was slowing to 180 knots. We both felt we were high although the PDI indicated only slightly above glideslope. We continued to configure the aircraft and called for the landing checklist. At 500; I called 'stable' when we realized the flaps were at 25 not 30. The Captain selected flaps 30 and we continued the approach and landing without incident.This was the Captain's third flight since March; I have flown more recently but it's been 3 weeks since my most recent flight.
column123: Visual approach night VMC to [Runway] XXR ZZZ.  PAPI inop. Upon accepting the visual approach from a modified right base to XXR we elected to follow the LNAV and VNAV path to the runway in visual conditions. Upon turning final we appeared to be high visually but VNAV was showing on path. We configured the aircraft rapidly; but on speed for gear down and final flaps.Upon reaching 500 feet both First Officer and I realized we were at flaps 25 not 30. I selected flaps 30 below 500 feet and we continued the approach and landing without incident.

column121: Coming into Scottsdale from ZZZ; we were cleared direct to DSERT for the DSERT2 RNAV (Area Navigation) arrival; then given instructions to cross YOLOW at 7;600 ft. and 250 kts. I put the altitude and speed in the FMS (Flight Management System) and the PF (Pilot Flying) set the AAD to 7;600 ft. and initiated the descent. Prior to reaching YOLOW; we acquired the runway at SDL and informed Approach; who simply acknowledged our transmission. We crossed YOLOW at 7;600 ft. and 250 kts; and shortly after were cleared for the visual approach to Runway 21 and instructed to contact Tower. The PF (Pilot Flying) began slowing the aircraft by reducing throttle and extending speed brakes while I activated the secondary flight plan containing the DSERT visual Runway 21 approach and activated the leg from YOLOW to EEDGR. The PF put 3;400 ft. into the AAD and activated VS (Vertical Speed) and dialed in a descent rate to reach 3;400 ft. at EEDGR. The PF directed me to extend the gear to assist with the descent and speed. Just prior to EEDGR we received a SINK RATE alert; and the PF shallowed the descent slightly; so we crossed EEDGR at approximately 200 kts and 3;600 ft. At the appropriate speeds; I extended the flaps; and shortly above 1;000 ft. AGL (Above Ground Level) we received a TERRAIN PULL UP alert; at which point I called for a go-around; which the PF executed. We climbed to 3;000 ft; ran the appropriate checklists; and then made a normal visual approach and landing.As we crossed EEDGR; the PF dialed the AAD to TDZE (Touch Down Zone); allowing the aircraft to continue descending while we attempted to get on profile.The PF and I were both distracted attempting to configure the aircraft and meet the profile of the approach. We both felt that the altitude and airspeed assignment at YOLOW made the switch to the REQUIRED visual approach with RNAV waypoints very difficult; and if we had simply flown a normal visual approach (which we've been doing for 15 years); we would have been fine. We were unable to configure early due to the ATC (Air Traffic Control) clearance; and the extra altitude carried past YOLOW forced a potentially unsafe descent rate. In the future; I will refuse a clearance to cross YOLOW at 7;600 ft and 250 kts if I expect to fly the visual to Runway 21. I feel the [Company] procedure as published actually reduces safety by drawing one or both pilots' attention into the cockpit during a critical phase of flight.

As the flying pilot I got totally sucked into my display and did [not] configure in time for a stabilized approach and decided at 900 AGL to initiate [a] go-around. Go-around was uneventful.Having not flown a lot in the last couple months I allowed myself to get distracted with the display and lost overall situational awareness in regards to proper aircraft configuration.

column121: We were cleared for a visual approach Runway XXL in ZZZ instructed to maintain 3000 ft. [until] ZZZZZ and 170 kts to follow traffic. Weather was clear and we were admiring the view and doing some sightseeing when we realized that the approach was not armed which kept us high on the approach. The PF (Pilot Flying) immediately disconnected the autopilot and called for the extension of landing gear and soon after lowered flaps on speed to get back on the approach. Tower approved S turns and we did two turns before getting on final course. PF had positive control the whole time as we landed without any incident and proceeded to our gate. At the gate we had a long discussion on our mistakes and the importance of the Stabilized Approach Criteria as it is emphasized in [Operations Manual] and [Flight Manual]. We agreed that a go-around would [have] been warranted.Be extra aware of complacency especially during nice weather. Always keep Stabilized Approach Criteria in mind; [be] ready to Go Around if it's not [met].

column121: ILS approach into ZZZ. I briefed the approach with course and frequency and confirmed the points in the FMS. Vectors to final were activated and I missed the course was not set on the inbound and I think when I briefed the frequency I was reading the frequency from NAV 2. We turned inbound and not seeing the errors I told the PNF that he had control; but this time we were well above the GS. We notified ATC that we were going to go missed and were given new instructions. After the missed; I reset the inbound course and reset the frequency. I then told the PNF that I had control and the next approach was done to a safe landing.I haven't flown in several months and the other pilot was his second flight in this type aircraft. I believe was a contributing factor on this approach.
column123: While being vectored to the ILS for Runway XX; the crew noticed that the localizer failed to auto-tune when the approach was armed. When it was determined that the course was not going to be captured the pilot transferred the auto pilot to the pilot monitoring side where the localizer did tune correctly. However; by this time the aircraft had flown through the course. The autopilot attempted to capture the course; but completed one or two 'S' turns. The crew agreed that the approach was not stabilized and we indicated to ATC that we intended to go-around and try the approach again. We were given vectors and an altitude and were cleared for the approach again. The following approach to landing was completed without issue.  Crew should have been more vigilant to ensure that the correct NAV frequency was tuned and the decision to transfer the coupled side autopilot could have been made earlier.

column121: IOE (initial operating) student was attempting a flaps 22 landing. During the approach; gusty winds from outer bands of an approaching hurricane contributed to an unstable approach and generating two glide slope EGPWS annunciations. We initiated a missed approach and were put in sequence to reattempt the approach. The vectors from the second approach brought us in above the glideslope. We engaged the vs (vertical speed) mode initially at 1;200 feet per minute (per GOM) and then 1;000 feet per minute as we closed in on the glide slope and stabilized approach criteria. The student had NAV armed; not APP; which as PM (pilot monitoring) I should have caught. My attention was briefly diverted to reassessing our fuel; which was 200 pounds over final divert at the final approach fix. At 1;000; instruments cross checked; the GS (glideslope) was exact (though not captured). At this point; we had been in visual conditions for several minutes. The runway did not have visual guidance (PAPI nor VASI); I looked out the window to verify the runway was clear; of which I annunciated. At approximately 700 feet AGL; we fell below the glide slope; generating additional glide slope EGPWS annunciations. Due to the winds; fuel nature; incoming weather; and diversion airport expecting similar conditions; I used my command authority to take control of the airplane; reestablish ourselves on the glide path; and land without further incident.

column121: The Captain had not flown in 5 months; and I am still relatively new to the airplane while only sporadically flying until this month. The bulletin for this issue was issued during a time that there were many outside factors affecting everyone's lives and then most of us did not fly very much. I feel that a bulletin as important as this should also be listed on the Approach Checklist to remind the crews. On the arrival to ZZZ; we were cleared direct to the airport from over 100 nautical miles away. ZZZ approach then kept us higher than normal. The Captain as the pilot flying attempted to descend and configure with speed brakes. We were given a tight base turn and cleared for the visual to XXR. Once it was obvious that we would not be stable by 1;000 feet AGL; the Captain called for a go-around. As I was notifying ATC; he proceeded to push the thrust levers to TOGA and then return them to MCT. The airplane immediately began an aggressive pitch up from about 2 degrees to over 25 degrees in less than 3 seconds. It was at that point that I remembered the bulletin for this aircraft in regards to the CG being less than 30.0%. The Captain disconnected the autopilot and lowered the nose. We circled back for XXR and landed without event.

column121: I was on an IFR direct flight plan. Since I was in 5/5 VMC conditions; and there was no other observed traffic in the vicinity of the airport; I cancelled IFR approximately 10-12 miles from the airport after having it in sight. I elected to enter the pattern on an extended left base for landing on Runway XX and made the appropriate radio call on the CTAF frequency. While on base; I began to descend below 1;032 feet MSL (the approximate normal pattern altitude) to approximately 700 feet MSL and initiated a left turn to final; approximately 2 miles from the XX runway threshold. As I made the turn to final; I allowed my altitude to fall to approximately 400 feet MSL (this was directly over the waterway; so it was also approximately 400 feet AGL). I made my final approach radio call and upon realizing I was somewhat low and below the desired glidepath on final. I initiated a correction and maintained that altitude until I was able to intercept the correct glidepath and had a red and white indication on the airport Runway XX PAPI. At that point I was perhaps a little over a mile from the airport threshold. From there I proceeded to a normal final approach and landing. My flight hours shown above are approximate; I am away from home as I file this report and thus do not have access to my logbook.

column121: Upon arriving at MSO airport on [Date] at approximately XA:00 the winds were not favorable to execute the ILS to RWY (Runway) 12. The weather conditions to perform the RNAV GPS D were alternating between ceiling and visibility going below minimum conditions. We were communicating with Dispatch along the way and were discussing diverting to our first alternate; which [was] Billings (BIL). The ceiling and visibility at MSO was above minimums at time of arrival. We started the RNAV approach with a circle to land on RWY 30. After crossing the final approach fix at 6;800 ft.; and descending to our MDA of 4;700 ft.; we started to have some ground contact. The FO (First Officer) said that he saw the PAPI lights to the right of us. The approach did not allow for us to circle to land northeast of RWY 12/30. The only way to commence the circle was to fly over the airport and circle around to the west. We briefly discussed circling to the west and quickly decided that it would be unsafe to circle around the airport at night in low visibility with LLWS (Low Level Wind Shear) conditions in mountainous terrain. During the decision making phase I had inadvertently disconnected the auto throttle. We continued to the missed approach point. As we went around I had realized that the MAP fix was not in the box. As we looked at the approach plate and quickly made a climbing left turn on a heading of about 340. We asked ATC for a heading while we headed in the proper direction. Once WENKE was back in the box we realized we were right on course.At approximately 8;000 ft. we were back in radar contact and was giving a vector off the final approach course. ATC asked us if we wanted to commence another approach or divert to a different airport. After discussing the conditions that we saw and weather forecast to be deteriorating; we unanimously decided that it would be unsafe to conduct a circle to land night approach based on the current weather and forecasted conditions.We then told ATC that we were going to divert to BIL. We sent ACARS message to Dispatch telling them we were diverting as well. After landing at BIL; Dispatch told us that we were to refuel and proceed back to MSO. Dispatch then said the weather was not good enough to go back and was delaying our departure. After waiting about an hour Dispatch said the winds were improving and we should be able to do the ILS RWY 12 back into MSO. We departed BIL for MSO with BIL as our alternate if we could not get in. We executed the ILS to RWY 12. While in Billings we contacted ATC and they told us at that time that they did not have radar until about 8;000 ft. The controller said that he was not working at that time but discussed with the previous controller that we were right where we were supposed to be and we said we just were at a high work load and wanted to verify with them that we were on course.High workload with low ceilings; LLWS; at night in mountainous terrain. Having to circle with visibility below what was projected and inadvertently disconnected auto throttles to land.I feel like this approach is not a safe approach to do at night. I spoke with several carriers that land at this airport and this approach is not authorized.

column121: On short final to land Runway XXR we heard a 'too low; flaps' audible warning. As the PM; I saw we were at flaps 25 with a planned flaps 30. I called this out to the First Officer who was the flying pilot as I selected flaps 25 and performed the landing check which we had apparently missed on approach. We made a normal landing. This was our second day in a row of very early flights with wake up calls and 3 time zone changes. While I didn't feel fatigued; I suspect I was more tired than I realized. We had just flown a nearly 5 hour cross country flight with a breakfast of a couple spoonfuls of granola; a couple spoonfuls of yogurt; and a few grapes. This was our company provided meal for the flight. While I normally take responsibility for providing my own food; this is not always possible in the current COVID environment. I believe this was a significant contributing factor. In hindsight; the proper response would have been to perform a go around and set up for another approach. While I don't feel my handling of the situation was unsafe; it was not consistent with company procedures. With reasonable sleep and nutrition I'd like to think I would have made a different decision.

column121: My first trip 'solo' as CO [Commanding Officer] was going well. I had a good crew and we performed well as a crew. On arrival after receiving vectors for approach to Runway XXL and established; the PF was looking around - sighting...flaps were at 15...I was focused on the required speed and frequency change at ZZZZZ; and the Relief Pilot was daydreaming as well.  At 1;000 feet the PF called 'stable'...and I saw his speed and ILS tracking were spot-on...and then we both realized at the same time that the gear was still UP and the flaps were at 15!!  He called gear down and flaps 25; which I did out of habit as response to the fallout.  I realized then; at the same time he did; that the approach was NOT going to work; at which point we both called 'go-around'.  The go-around was at 800 feet AFL and was UGLY!!  Go-arounds from this altitude are often UGLY!!  The subsequent vectors and second approach went as it should and we landed safely...the desired outcome.  The debrief at the gate was thorough and each of us contributed; as well as took appropriate blame for our individual contribution to such an embarrassing and actually humiliating event.We each had recently been through a simulator check.  The Relief Pilot and I (CO) each having less than 40 hours in type; and the FO having just returned from a 7 month period of not flying.  Combined total flight time of this 3-man crew exceeded X0;000 hours.  However on this day we were each victims of a few different causal factors:  fatigue...we had just flown XA+45 hours from ZZZZ; neither of us slept well the previous night; and we were each the victims of relative inactivity due to COVID-related restrictions; each of these led us to be distracted; lackadaisical; and only partially effective as a crew.Causal factors contributing to this outrageously ridiculous display of airman-ship are deemed to be at least the following: longer layovers; [lack of] more well established activity schedules during our layovers-even during times of social restrictions; [lack of] better food onboard flights for crews; [lack of] better procedural discipline is possibly another even considering each of us had been through simulator training very recently.
