column121: On Aircraft X to ZZZ; we were given a turn to base for a visual approach to XR inside the final approach fix 600 feet above the FAF.  We realized we weren't going to be able to complete the approach under the stable approach criteria so I called the go-around at 1;000 feet AGL. Once we got the aircraft climbing I called ATC saying that we are going around. ATC said climb and maintain 3000 feet; we were already climbing and had a high rate of climb because we only had about XX people on board.  While we were cleaning up the aircraft and setting up the FCP; I realized our Climb rate was high and we were approaching 3;000 feet quickly. I said 'watch your altitude' and asked ATC if we could get 3;500 feet. ATC denied it and said to maintain 3;000 feet and contact departure. Unfortunately; the correction of bringing the nose down and power back happened too late and we climbed through our altitude by about 200 feet. The correction came and we corrected our altitude back to 3;000 feet. On the second attempt approach tried to do the same thing to us again however we denied it and asked to be vectored for the ILS.  ATC turned us to intercept the final approach course too close and I told them we won't be able to do it due to us having a tailwind; and that we are going to need to intercept it from the other side. I told ATC the intercept we would need. On that attempt we completed the approach successfully and landed with no further complications.Due to having a poor vector for the approach; and no warning of a close approach; we ended up high on a visual approach to Runway XR. For that reason we decided to go-around. The altitude assigned to us was only about 1;200 feet above the altitude we decided to go-around at. By the time we got the aircraft configured for the go around and contacted ATC we were even closer to that altitude. Due to having a high workload in that environment; a light weight; and being already close to the altitude assigned to us the climb rate was too high when I made the call out to the Captain. I also tried to rectify the situation before it happened by asking ATC for a higher altitude but was denied.Deny the visual approach and ask ATC to just fly the ILS to give us more time. Say unable instead of asking for a higher altitude.
column123: Today on Aircraft X to ZZZ we were given a turn inside of the FAF for Runway XR into ZZZ. ZZZ Approach had us at 3;000 feet. The FAF is 2;400 feet. We were unable to widen the approach due to a snowstorm to the west and had been cleared for the visual. We were too high; so we completed a missed approach. On the missed we went above our assigned altitude of 3;000 feet by 200 feet. We requested higher but were rejected; so we descended the 150-200 feet. On the second attempt; approach tried to give us the same turn at the same altitude. We asked for the ILS. We were again given a bad vector; however; farther out. We were given a 060 heading to intercept. Due to a 40 knot tailwind we flew through and requested a 120 heading to join. It was accepted. We landed successfully.We got a bad vector inside the final approach fix while 600 feet above the FAF altitude. We were given no indication or expectation of a short approach. We were too high and too fast for our approach. We conducted a go-around and subsequently climbed 150-200 feet high.

column121: Lost SA partially on arrival. Approach gave us a 240 heading and asked us to call the field. I had selected managed speed out of 10;000 and was configuring. Extended gear descending through 9;200; so we could continue to slow. Aircraft wasn't slowing. Captured LOC; but overshot a bit. Glide slope didn't capture. Once I saw the PAPI I saw I was low I leveled; continued to configure and slow. Leveled at 1;000 feet AFL momentarily to rejoin glideslope and ensure configured for stabilized approach. Had runway in sight the whole time after turning final; met gates at 1;000 feet AFL and 500 feet AFL; but felt behind and should have gone around. Maintenance delay; unfamiliar airport; late runway change; long day (nearly 9 hours of flying); no approach control (ZZZ Center handling approach due to COVID outage).I should have asked for vectors onto the approach rather than accepting visual. ZZZ is surrounded by high terrain; I was unfamiliar even though I felt I had prepared carefully; vectors would have provided a less dynamic environment. Also; if I were in a similar situation; I would go-around and ensure I don't press when I feel at all like I have lost SA. Also; the high altitude made slowing the aircraft down more difficult. I didn't include that in my briefing. I know better; but after the long day I failed to consider that as a factor.
column123: ATC gave us a 90 degree plus vector for Runway XXR and asked us to call the field. We had it in sight and were cleared for the visual. We were above the glideslope; with flaps 1; put the gear down to slow down; and used managed speed. The approach was armed; caught; but overshot the localizer because the aircraft wasn't slowing down. The glideslope didn't capture and we were then low on the glide slope as we passed through it from above. We leveled off; slowed down and finished configuring. We were within stable criteria at 1;000 and 500 feet; but it was a messy approach.Cause - Long duty day; extended due to maintenance. Bad vector from ATC and pressure to go visual. Approach was closed and Denver center was handling approach.Don't be pressured into accepting a visual at night with mountainous terrain. Go-around if an approach is feeling uncomfortable. Slow down earlier when at a higher elevation airport.

column121: Being vectored onto the final approach course 26R; we had a brief encounter with a wake turbulence. And then again during the landing flare to the point where it was becoming a problem to maintain a stabilized approach path above the runway in ground effect. Did not have time to notify air traffic control that this happened. I don't know if we were behind a heavy previous arrival. But we were getting really close to having to go around from the landing flare. The roll motion was becoming such a problem with keeping the aircraft stabilized; and aligned with the runway. I was actually shocked that we touchdown as smooth as we did. I asked the pilot flying if he thought that was a bit unusual his statement was 'I was just one more jolt away from going around'. Wake turbulence encounters in Atlanta are becoming an every week problem; more like almost every inbound leg into Atlanta problem. Also it seems there is little consideration given to the fact that we may be departing behind something that might be a threat as well. I'm beginning to think that there's nothing much going to be done about it until something serious happens. Often the wake encounter threats are not much of a problem if there's sufficient crosswind push the wake away from trailing aircraft; from my experience it does not take much to all. However I've learned that this is still a problem at some point on the arrival or departure procedure. For example a crosswind on the departure runways will clear you of the wake threat for takeoff; but might present a very real problem in trail of a heavy navigating to a point; to the same on that departure or arrival. Especially if it's a direct head; or tailwind now.
column122: Reporter stated wake encounters in ATL are becoming far too frequent and he recommended a change to arrival and departure procedures to increase spacing.

column121: The First Officer was flying a visual approach to ZZZ Runway XXR with right traffic from ZZZZZ3 arrival. Radar vector to final; cleared to 3;000 feet from 4;000 feet and cleared the visual. First Officer was in vertical speed and I set 3;000 feet in the MCP window. He armed approach mode and captured the localizer. The aircraft leveled at 3;000 feet and did not capture the glide slope as we were too high. Gear was down; flaps 20 and speed was 163. First Officer disconnected the autopilot and dove down to capture the glide slope. I suggested speed brakes; which he used to get down. This rapid descent caused the Tower to issue an altitude warning. I then became very busy stowing speed brakes; landing flaps; checklists; and verifying stable approach parameters. We never did the 1;000 feet call for set M/A altitude and cleared to land check (which we were). The event; although satisfying the stable approach parameters; was highly irregular and borderline go-around scenario. The debrief included mostly what went wrong and why. We discussed and I noted the main errors. The First Officer said not flying for 7 months was the issue.

column121: On approach to ZZZ; the ATIS was obtained with reported winds of 340 @ 15 G 28. The airport was configured to land east; utilizing Runways XL and XXC. The First Officer (FO) and I discussed how this was odd and not desirable as the tailwind component with gusts was at our 10 knot limitation. Additionally; the ATIS stated there were wind shear advisories in the area. On final approach inside the FAF; maintaining target airspeed within Vref +10 was extremely difficult due to the high wind gusts; shifting winds; and shear. As pilot flying; my airspeed was hovering at +10 with occasional jumps up to +20. The wind vector on the MFD was showing winds at 030 between 20-35 knots until 100 feet or so; where the winds sheared to 340 at an unknown speed. I touched down within the touchdown zone but off centerline by approximately 10 feet. Taxi to the hanger was uneventful and no damage was noted. During the debrief; the FO and I agreed a go-around with a request to land on a Westerly direction should have been made. The cause of this event were gusty winds; poor traffic flow decision by ATC; and undesirable speed control by the Pilot Flying due to windshear.  ATC decision to land to the east with reported winds of 340 @ 15 G 28. This decision by ATC caused an unsafe tailwind condition when coupled with very strong and gusty crosswinds. Additionally; myself or the FO should have requested a landing to the west. However; the First Officer and I determined that any request would be denied as ZZZ has a reputation for not accommodating these types of request in order to maintain efficient traffic flows. There is pressure to land with the prevailing flow of traffic at a major airport like ZZZ when the crew sees other aircraft landing in the existing conditions; even if the crew would prefer an alternate runway. I strongly believe ATC created an unsafe situation that may have inadvertently pressured pilots into landing with a tailwind in gusty conditions. When I made the decision to conduct the approach; a go around would have been the correct action when the wind sheared at 100 feet.

column121: Following two previous approach attempts into ZZZ1 followed by diversions to our Filed Alternates; on [consecutive evening and morning flights] of Aircraft X. I was advised by Dispatch that if the Crew was willing; they would like us to fly back to ZZZ1 and make yet a third attempt. I immediately said I did not feel safe operating the flight as I didn't feel the approach was safe based on the current conditions at the airport; and the weather we had experienced during the last two attempts. I then stated I would not be willing to make another attempt. During the first attempt (Aircraft X ZZZ-ZZZ1) we experienced moderate turbulence; gusty winds; heavy rain; in IMC at night followed by a Windshear Warning and Escape Maneuver at 150 ft. AGL; following this up with a consecutive approach the next morning (Aircraft X ZZZ2-ZZZ1) resulting in an early Go-Around / Diversion to alternate due to Heavy Rain; Moderate to Severe Turbulence on Approach; Strong Crosswinds on Final; Changing (Increasing) Gusting Wind Conditions on the surface and the previous aircraft making a Severe Turbulence Encounter Report on Final with the Controller inquiring as to passenger injuries on board. This led to a phone call from my direct Supervisor; who tried to convince me the operation was safe. I clearly stated to both persons; that our Dispatch Release reads; and I quote.. 'By Signing this Dispatch Release we are affirming our belief that this flight can be made safely'. I was very clear that after my previous two attempts; I did not feel comfortable operating the flight. The Initial Plan was for us to depart in just 30 minutes after refueling; which again I stated I did not feel safe operating the flight. Followed by yet another phone call from my Supervisor who reviewed the weather with me. Explaining things were much better now and we can depart. Again; I expressed my concerns as to the safety of the flight based on recent experience in the storm system; and inaccuracies experienced with the past weather reports that allowed us to dispatch prior. I felt I was being pushed to operate the flight. My Supervisor continued to tell me of other carriers that made it into ZZZ1 today. Once again I explained they didn't deal with what we had to do; and again relayed I was not comfortable making a flight to ZZZ1 today. After some discussion; it seemed my Supervisor was becoming agitated stating that if I didn't fly he would have to. I remained silent as this was not pertinent to my decision. I was again asked to operate - to which I repeated my opinion that I didn't feel it was a safe operation and I would not even go there as a passenger if you asked me to at that time. I was then told I was being pulled from the flight; I was being relieved of all duty pending an investigation and I would hear from someone in the company. The phone call was then disconnected by the other party.I feel this was an attempt to force a flight crew to accept a flight that I had clearly stated I didn't feel it was safe to operate. I feel that this is a violation of the Captain's joint Authority with Dispatch to operate a flight and is in direct conflict with PIC's (Pilot in Command) responsibility and authority. It also seems to me to be in conflict with the creation of a 'Just Culture: required be FAA in implementing a SMS. A pilot should never be coerced to accept a mission he/she feels is unsafe. In this situation; the PIC was being asked to Dispatch into an area where an URGENT PIREP was broadcast via Center Frequencies advising of Moderate to Severe Turbulence reported below 11;000 ft. by an aircraft on Final Approach to ZZZ1. Another aircraft should not be dispatched to fly an approach in Severe Turbulence. The most basic principle is Dispatchers; Managers; and Supervisors need to listen to the people under their charges. Air Carriers need to stress the importance of Safety; by not just stating they have a culture based on Safety; but by actually exercisingone especially when performance metrics may not be met but safety goals will be achieved. As Pilots; I feel we are all mission-oriented; but the need to complete the mission can never exceed operational safety.

column121: Day VMC. Conducted initial approach [Runway] XXR. PF (Pilot Flying) called missed approach into ZZZ [Runway] XXR due to windshear with airspeed loss below 500 ft. AFE (Above Field Elevation) with advancement in override thrust. Subsequently received windshear caution. After selecting TOGA and pitch up flew flight director. No windshear escape guidance provided as the windshear was not sustained. Thrust was advanced to GA (Go-Around) RSV as a precaution. Stable in the missed approach maneuver with no caution windshear; PF called Flaps 4 due to flaps full configured. Gear retracted at a safe altitude with positive climb rate. Flew ATC assigned runway heading with missed approach climb profile. Subsequent approach flown to the ILS profile with Flaps 5. Flew the glide slope to RWY [Runway] XXR. No windshear event occurred. Received wind report with gust factor 28 kts. Selected manual speeds to increase approach sped until 4-5 kts. until after passing 500 ft. AFE 'stable' call the target speed was reduced. Maintained visual descent rate with visual cues and instrumentation of the ILS. During round out to flare automation set the aural 'sink rate' message. FO (First Officer) announced correcting due to the aural call and confirmed with a cross check the sink rate was induced by maintaining descent rate with the descent maneuver to coupled round out pitch rate simultaneously for the landing maneuver; not due to windshear as the speed was stable. FO and CA (Captain) on debrief both acknowledged the aural alert at post flight and both agreed with given conditions the approach to landing was stable given the conditions. Touchdown was within LTP (Landing Threshold Point) distance.Second approach with Flaps 5 configuration allowed for a higher approach speed and acknowledging the wind factor with increase in target speed for gusty conditions allowed for less susceptibility for the same condition. Automation with sensor inputs from other automated systems is subject to its own set design limitations and may not reflect positive confirmation of the event with pilot judgment of the same situation monitoring the same event. Such as automation latencies detecting the windshear event prior to the windshear caution; or sink rate alert while the pilot is initiating real time assertive maneuvers in turbulent conditions. Inertial vertical speed descent rates measure acceleration as it filters out noise of turbulence conditions causing a lag as opposed to real time judgment of the flight crew.
column123: Approach into ZZZ on our 1st attempt we conducted a missed approach due to wind shear. On the 2nd approach in the flare we briefly went to a sink rate of 1;100 [ft. per minute] the FO (First Officer) was already correcting and we received a 'sink rate' aural tone just prior to touchdown.

I hadn't flown in a month so my situational awareness was not on a level it should have been and I should have called a go-around.

column121: While on the RNAVM XX in ZZZ1 we encountered a GPWS event. At the beginning of the approach we had a tailwind and the descent angle of the approach caused us to be a little fast starting the approach. As the pilot monitoring I should have caught this issue sooner and brought it to the attention of the PF (Pilot Flying). Because of the speed the PF disconnected the autopilot/auto throttles and slowed the plane to the proper approach speed. During the turn onto final the PF was visual with the runway and let the nose drop which caused a momentary don't sink. He corrected and the warning went away. Still with the airport visual he tried to correct on the VASI and received another momentary don't sink call at around 700 ft. We continued the approach and the landing was normal. I don't have anything to add for [this report].I have learned many lessons from this very short flight. I haven't had much ZZZ experience and my FO (First Officer) who was PF has had very little. We tried to mitigate the possible issues by having a slow cruise speed of 210 kts. and trying to get ready for the approach early. We briefed the 3.4 GP and felt we were in a good position for the approach. With the unfamiliarity of the situation and the weather in ZZZ this day I missed the strong tailwind that was making our situation worse. This put us behind and we had to play catch up. It was turbulent the whole flight and when he disconnected the AP/AT my [situational awareness] went down because I was worried about his flight path at that time. As I look back at the event and if I could do this again I would have us discontinue the approach early and come around to try again. Once we continued and got out of the [weather] and had the runway visually climbing back into it in that terrain did not seem the safest option. But if we had bailed early it would have made the situation easier the second time.

column121: I was the PNF and the First Officer on this flight to ZZZ. On our approach into ZZZ we were slightly high on vectors and then cleared for the visual approach. The Captain PF was at a speed of 230 knots indicated airspeed (KIAS) and was trying to descend and slow to land. He was unable to slow to 200 KIAS as we were entering the Class C airspace and we determined the approach was unstable and executed a go-around. We entered the traffic pattern and landed successfully. I believe the cause is a decrease in proficiency due to reduced flying during the pandemic. We were doing 220-230 KIAS while entering the Class C airspace.

column121: During arrival to HAN; I was a jumpseat observer. We were at 2;000 ft. on the localizer; cleared for the ILS [Runway] 11 Left approach. Just outside the Final Approach Fix; I heard the Captain (Pilot Flying); say glideslope captured; and he set the missed approach altitude of 5;000 ft. in the altitude alerter. I was confused; because looking at the FO's (First Officer's) PFD (Primary Flight Display); I could see that we were below the glideslope; and it was no captured. I could not see the Captain's PFD to verify whether or not the glideslope was captured. The aircraft started descent from 2;000 ft.; even though we were not on the glideslope. I called out to check the glideslope and/or altitude. I did not have a hot mike; and maybe they weren't hearing me. The aircraft passed the Final Approach Fix approximately 500 ft. low. I called out the glideslope deviation again. The other jumpseat observer was also telling them to climb; and I yelled 'Go Around'. Then we got a GS caution with a line strike through the FMA on the PFD. At approximately 1;200 ft.; the go around was finally initiated with full scale deflection indicated on FO's PFD.During the surprise go around; we were instructed to maintain heading and climb to 5;000 ft. At some point the autopilot was disconnected; and situational awareness was deteriorating. The aircraft was veering to the right of track; 20 degrees nose up; and not following the flight director commands of 10 degrees nose up. I called out something like 'Who's flying the airplane; fly the airplane!' The flying pilot then steered back toward the flight director; regained control and the autopilot was re-engaged. After reaching 5;000 ft. we were vectored back for another approach to Runway 11 Left.Also of note; there was runway/taxiway construction at HAN airport that could have possibly created a glideslope anomaly. Though I could not see the Captain's flight instruments; it is possible there may have been a loss of glideslope indication at the start of the event. On the FO's PFD; I could see that the ILS was identified; and localizer/glideslope were displayed at all times.The Pilot Monitoring should have noticed on his instruments that the aircraft was not on glideslope; and should have called out the deviation immediately. When I saw the deviation; I was trying to alert the crew to fix the situation. I should have called out go around immediately.
column123: While inbound on the localizer for RNWY (Runway) 11L; the glideslope was captured and the 'G/S' FMA was active. Shortly after capturing the glideslope; the 'G/S' FMA blinked and had a yellow strikethrough. The glideslope then started to fall beneath us; while the autopilot tried to increase the rate of descent. The Pilot Flying switched to V/S [Vertical Speed]; and rolled in more V/S down to stay on glideslope with a more than normal descent rate. I instructed to 'level off; level off' followed by 'climb back to 2;000 ft. It was then I noticed the glideslope dot had blinked and reappeared at two dots high; indicating we were low. The Captain in the observer seat then said 'Go Around; Go Around'. The Pilot Flying initiated the go around with the 'TOGA' [Takeoff Go-Around] switches and sometime thereafter turned off the autopilot. He then hand flew the go around to 5;000 ft. and present heading. After notifying Tower of our missed approach; we were told to contact Approach frequency. During this event; with my seat position; I was only able to observe the Captain's PFD [Primary Flight Display]. After landing safely and gathering all facts observed between crewmembers; it was apparent that the autopilot tried to capture a false glideslope and failed; thus giving us the yellow strikethrough the 'G/S' FMA."

column121: I as Pilot Flying; was initially on a stable approach descending to 3;500 ft. on base for [landing]. I had briefed a Flaps 22 landing. As I was turning base to final (a left turn) and starting further descent; the winds shifted and resulted in a loss of tailwind; and my airspeed increased in the turn. At that same time Tower told us to turn 10 degrees right I believe due to traffic. Through all of this; my approach became unstable with too steep a descent; and got [we] a GPWS sink rate alert. I immediately executed a go around. ATC issued a new clearance and we came back and landed without incident. Suggest some more insight; and different scenarios taught on Flaps 22 landings possibly during training. Because of the higher airspeed vs. Flaps 45; it seems there is an increased chance of unstable approaches; especially if the winds shift as in my case. I carried too much power on this approach and also got distracted with the instruction from Tower to turn opposite of my base to final turn.

column121: ATIS was advising ILS approaches in use. We requested the RNAV Y for pilot proficiency reasons. The request was approved by the final approach controller. At the end of the RNAV arrival; we were put on the published heading and told to expect vectors onto final for the RNAV Y approach by the final approach controller. We requested but were denied direct both initial approach fixes to start the approach. We were given 2 vectors; the last being [Heading] 290 and the clearance to fly heading 290 maintain 4;300 ft. until established on the final approach course cleared for the RNAV Y approach as we were vectored toward the course between the initial approach fixes. The approach became challenging at that point because the final approach course and glidepath anticipation cues were not visible and therefore we were unable to arm the approach. Additionally; the 4;300 ft. restriction left us high rather than leave us in a position to descend to 2;900 ft. in the space between the initial approach fixes because the width of the lateral course at that point is much narrower than a localizer signal. The IMC and terrain rich environment added to the concerns at this point. I feel that part of this challenge was created by the technique US air traffic controllers are using to get aircraft onto RNAV approaches. Rather that clear an aircraft to a fix on the approach; they are trying to vector them onto final as they would for an aircraft flying an ILS approach. My first safety concern is that there is not final approach course to intercept when flying an RNAV approach such as the localizer transmitting out bound from the runway for many miles. Rather there seems to be courses from one fix to the next all the way down the published approach. When ATC says 'fly heading xxx until established on the final approach' there isn't a final approach to intercept. I question whether a clearance of that wording is procedurally correct on RNAV approach. There are no examples I can find in company manuals or the Airman Information Manual the depicts starting an RNAV approach at anywhere other than a fix on the approach. I have had this type of vector onto final clearance for an RNAV approach for RNAV approaches in other U.S. destinations also so I feel the safety threat posed by this type of RNAV clearance is widespread in the USA. I have never received a clearance like that outside of the USA. Outside the USA; I have always been cleared to fix to start the RNAV approach. My second safety concern is that if that type of RNAV approach clearance is correct; we do not train that at any point during initial or recurrent training. All of our simulator training clears the aircraft to a fix on the approach when cleared for an RNAV approach. If the type of ATC clearance we got is procedurally correct we never see it in training.
column125: Air Carrier pilot reported ATC issued a vector to intercept the final approach course for an RNAV approach which does not have a final approach course. The reporter stated this type of clearance is issued routinely by U.S. ATC but overseas they are always issued direct to a fix to begin the approach.

column121: On arrival into ZZZ from the south; we were vectored off of the ZZZZZ procedure and held high due to traffic. We had been previously queried as to our RVR requirements for [Runway] XXR. We were set up and briefed for CATIII approach to XXR. Just prior to ZZZZZ waypoint; we heard traffic ahead cleared to descend via ZZZZZ arrival landing north. We were then given clearance to descend via the arrival landing north and were unable to meet the new crossing restrictions. Due to 71 knot crosswinds our heading was 320. The vector we were given for descent was 340. This was assumed to be a 360 for descent. As we turned through a heading of 260 ish we were told to make an immediate right turn for restricted airspace. We complied. We continued on the arrival and had an uneventful hand off to approach.I believe that the ATC is currently overworked due to COVID cutbacks. Many communications are being missed in the last months because ATC are covering too many sectors. Our lack of fore warnings regarding the changes to airport configuration left us with inadequate situational awareness. We followed the vectors given to us with incorrect assumptions. I'm grateful that this situation was resolved without incident. My recommendation is that we begin to re-staff ATC to normal pre-COVID operation.
column123: Flight was left high on the ZZZZZ Arrival.   ATIS had us landing on [Runway] XXR.   We were cleared for the ZZZZZX but never informed by ATC that ATIS X special was issued at XA22z we only learned that there was a runway change due from listing to arrival aircraft in front of us.   We were now high for landing [Runway] YYL by 6;000 feet at the same time we were instructed to slow to 190 kts.  We can slow down but not go down at the same time.   We were instructed to fly heading 340 as we descended the controller once again said turn left 340 which we were on and we assumed he wanted us to do a 360 which is common to loose altitude and rejoin the arrival.   He was busy with several other aircraft left high the weather was poor in mild turbulence.  The next call was for us to turn I believe 040 because he said we are going to hit a restricted area.I was heads down retrieving the new ATIS as well and programming the FMS for a different runway.    This was a combination of change of runways; poor weather; and aircraft being left high by two controllers and high winds with an approach to mins.   This was a series of events that lead to an aircraft being out of position.When the weather is poor we cannot skimp on air traffic controllers they need to be tentative to aircraft and where they are going.   If the aircraft is out of position.  It should be quickly realize especially when a runway change has been posted and an aircraft may not have received the special ATIS.

column121: While operating Aircraft X; we experienced loss of thrust in the right engine while we were leveling off at 8;000 ft on the ZZZZZ 2 Arrival into ZZZ1. Earlier that morning; when we started the engines in ZZZ. We noticed that the right engine was approaching a hung start; however; it stabilized while it was still within the limitations. The Captain and I confirmed that the engine had stabilized and all engine indications appeared normal. Everything remained normal until our arrival into ZZZ1. The Captain was Pilot Flying; and when he increased thrust to arrest our descent and level off at 8;000 ft; we noticed that the right engine did not respond to the thrust lever. The thrust remained around 30% N1 regardless of the thrust lever position. We then notified ATC and requested radar vectors. Once we were on vectors; we ran the QRH for 'Thrust Lever Inoperative' 1-34. After discussing the situation; we determined that since the thrust was remaining at idle; it was in the interest of reducing complexity and increasing safety to keep the right engine operating at idle. We then ran the 'Single Engine Approach and Landing'  QRH 1-27 and configured accordingly. We continued the approach and landed uneventfully. We advised ATC that we did not need assistance taxiing to the gate. Once we arrived at the gate; we performed the shutdown procedure. When we shut down the right engine; it took multiple attempts to get the thrust lever to shut the engine down. After completing the shutdown checklist; we advised Maintenance of the issue.

column121: Descending on the DSNEE 4 arrival from EMLLD; we became aware of congested airport environment with two different TCAS traffic alerts very close by. Approach had us at 190 knots departing DSNEE; and we were expecting the visual to runway 20R. We had gone to flaps 2 configuration. We were handed off late to the final controller; and when we switched over we heard a company call sign with nearly identical numeric as ours; cleared for the RNAV (RNP) Z runway 20R. We heard the other aircraft read back the clearance; we knew a VERY similar call sign was on frequency. We thought we might be cleared for the same approach. We checked in and asked if we were going to get the RNAV approach; and the controller said no; we were going to get a visual unless we asked for the approach. Shortly after; the controller cleared an aircraft to descend to 3000'; but neither of us were sure that it was our call sign. The First Officer (FO) attempted to clarify; but the controller was very busy and talking nonstop. We flew a few more miles; and before we could clarify he repeated the clearance to descend to 3000' and asked if we had the airport in sight. We called the airport; and said we were high. He cleared us to fly through final approach course and intercept from the right. FO read back the clearance; and we both agreed it was going to be tight. I immediately called for gear down and got the flaps to full; I also used the speed brakes as long as safely practical. We switched over to tower control; and I made the glideslope at approx. 1400' AGL; but we were still 40kts fast. I told the FO we were going to do a 'Soft Go'. At 1000' AGL I called for 'Go Around; Takeoff/around (TOGA)' and advanced the thrust levers to TOGA. I then called 'climb' as I brought the thrust levers back to what I thought was the climb position; but was actually the Maximum Continuous Thrust (MCT) detent. The tower initially held us to 2000'; so I reduced our climb rate - we were accelerating rapidly. As we cleaned up and were cleared to 3000'; the FO recognized that we were in MCT detent and brought it to my attention. By then we were clean and rapidly approaching 250 KIAS. I brought the thrust levers back to idle as we passed 260; and we saw a maximum speed of about 280 before we began to slow down again. I selected speed mode and set 220 KIAS; positioned the thrust levers in the climb detent and re-engaged auto thrust. We slowed and turned back into a right downwind for an uneventful visual approach and landing at SNA.Causes(1)	Late clearance to descend. SNA is challenging even when on a normal profile; and a few miles made all the difference.(2)	Task saturation. The L.A basin is one of the busiest airspaces in the world. Two Traffic Alerts and nonstop chatter from the approach controller indicated that he was task saturated; as well.(3)	Call sign confusion. The scheduling shop should never have had two call signs separated by one digit arriving at the same airport at the same time.(4)	Approach expectations. We were going to do a visual; and changed our setup and expectations when we heard preceding traffic approach clearance.(5)	Fatigue. Our FAR 117 duty day limit was 10 hours. We had been awake since 3.5 hours earlier to make sign-in; and were at 9:00 of our 10:00 limit when we arrived at SNA. When we knew we were too high to likely make a successful approach; we could have asked for a vector and more time/distance to descend. Flight scheduling needs to de-conflict call signs so very similar ones are not scheduled to arrive at the same airport at the same time. The ATC controllers seemed very busy; perhaps a few more controllers on-shift would help divide the workload better. Crew scheduling could alleviate fatigue factors by avoiding sequence builds that have crews flying into airports at the back end of their duty periods that are known to present unusual challenges (even if they aren't officially 'special qualification' airports).
column125: Airbus First officer reported a go-around caused by late clearance to descend and confusion caused by a similar call sign for an aircraft preceding them.

column119: Environment - Non Weather Related; Human Factors; Airport
column121: We started out vectored off our arrival by ZZZ Center to the southwest with several step down altitudes.  30 miles from the airport we found ourselves at 20;000 MSL which is about 10;000 feet higher than usual for a normal descent.  ATC; specifically ZZZ Center; was overloaded with aircraft going into south State X.  The Controller brought us around and eventually around 10;000 MSL he told us to go direct to the ZZZ airport.  At that time; we were about 15-18 miles away at just under 10;000 MSL.  A few minutes later the Controller asked if we had the field in sight to which we replied that we did; but we needed a vector because we were too high to safely attempt the visual approach.  We were given a left 360 degree turn and cleared down to 3;000 MSL also while given direct to the ZZZ airport.  Upon leveling off at 3;000 MSL we were in a cloud layer and couldn't see the field.  We requested lower and were given 1;500 MSL.  Once we reach that altitude; we could see the field and was cleared the visual approach to Runway X and to switch to Tower.On final; after fully configuring the aircraft; Tower told us to turn right to a heading of 180.  Very confused; we asked him to repeat the instructions at which point we executed a missed approach for a disabled aircraft on the runway.  Tower never actually told us to go-around.  We were given heading 180 and a climb to 4;000 MSL.  Following the level off we were instructed to hold present position however the aircraft will not hold present position on HDG mode.  It can only PPOS HOLD while on and active FMS leg with less than .25 NM cross track.  Knowing this; we told the Controller we needed a fix to hold and we were given the ZZZ2 NDB which doesn't exist in the database.  We instead requested the missed approach fix for the RNAV X approach.We weren't sure or told how long the runway was going to be shut down so the crew started to discuss diverting options which ZZZ1 was the only real viable option.  The FMS indicated it would take about 3;500 lbs of fuel and we had about 2;300lbs of reserve fuel on board.  From my quick calculation; I had to make a decision fairly soon considering the following:  we had about 5;700lbs on fuel on board; unknown runway closure; time; FMS calculation of 3;500 lbs of fuel required to ZZZ1; high volume of ZZZ Center traffic and high controller work load.  I sent a message to Dispatch informing them we would attempt one more approach and if unable to make it in we would divert to ZZZ1.We notified ZZZ Center Controller we needed to attempt another approach or we would soon have need to divert.  We were given vectors around and cleared for the visual to Runway X once again.  As the pilot flying; I opted to proceed direct to the FAF of the RNAV X and continue the visual approach.  At about the base leg portion I turned off the autopilot and turned towards the runway.  I believe that a slight turn inside of the FAF did not sequence the approach nor the glide path information which led to a position above the glide path.  I flew down to correct the glide path all while in and out of 1;000 FPM VVI.  I acknowledged the momentary deviations of descent and quickly corrected as I was now on the visual indications from the PAPI's.  On short final; a thermal briefly interrupted our descent and with a quick correction back to glide path I corrected the path for a safe landing.I believe the accumulation of all factors above could have led to a critically low fuel situation much quicker than expected.  I believe the saturation of airspace on a very busy travel day could have been underestimated in the current climate of understaffed controllers.  ZZZ Center controllers were so saturated with aircraft that any chance to hand an aircraft off to a visual approach whether it was really safe to do so or not was attempted.  Unsure about divert fuel and runway state created a high-importance; high stress moment of deciding when a divert should occur without having proper data supporting it.  Lastly; in a heightened sense of urgency with a tight turn inbound to the runway and sub-sequence 'Cutting the corner' led to the aircraft being in a position higher than the glide path.I would suggest the following for operations into ZZZ Captain only approach and landing.  I believe most captains take it upon themselves to make the approach and landing due to the short runway as it is.  There should be more 'hip pocket' information whether in the form of a bulletin or some other format that provides rough numbers of divert options; fuel required; routing; etc when a formal alternate is not filed.  I also suggest fuel planning take into account busy travel days and add as much fuel as practical for contingencies even on days with VMC conditions that does not preclude landing due to being over max landing weight.  I also believe during all forms of training (initial; recurrent; etc) special authorization airports and airports such as ZZZ are discussed. It is my suggestion that ZZZ be a mandatory training event.  I can tell you everything you need to know about flying to ZZZ2 yet we don't actually go there in the aircraft.  However; airports such as ZZZ are rarely mentioned when we fly there every week.  I suggest more 'hangar flying' where scenarios are discussed (such as this one) in a classroom/training environment to get captains to start thinking about scenarios such as these.  I'd also like to recommend that one or two approaches from ZZZ3 be included in our Jeppesen charts or company pages.  Should ZZZ be shut down for any reason and there is not enough divert fuel to safely make it back to the mainland there will be a 10;000 foot runway available; especially in IMC conditions.  Lastly; I suggest that pilots refrain from attempting to cut corners while approaching the runway from greater than 90 degrees.  Instead; they should fly the published approach in its entirety including glide slopes or glide paths so that they maintain a safe; stable approach to the runway.
column125: Air carrier Captain reported a disabled aircraft on intended landing runway resulted in their flight being vectored away from their approach path.  The additional vectoring caused them to be concerned with their fuel state and made a request to be cleared as soon as possible.  The crew was cleared for another approach and landing and reported being unstable/high before safely landing the aircraft.

column121: On final; Runway XXL; ZZZ; Night; 1000 foot ceiling; Me (Captain) flying; gusty winds; very light load; FO low currency- only 150 hrs in type since hired 1 year ago; and has not been flying much. I was hand flying at 2;000 feet on normal ILS. Called for gear down; flaps 15 at approximately 2;000 feet and flaps 30 landing; check at about 1500 feet. FO ran check list; while I hand flew the approach. The winds were gusty; our target was +15 ref and it was night.  I saw flap refs change on airspeed tape; and glanced at gear lights when FO ran checklist; but I did not have full attention to FO duties since it was dark and gusty. We broke out at 1;000 feet; I called clear to land and set missed approach altitude. At 500 feet we simultaneously got the 500 auto call out and Too Low Flaps. I simultaneously; started a Go-Around (finger to toga button) and looked at flap gauge - which was at 25. But I hesitated on the go-around when I saw why we got the GPWS. I commanded flaps set to 30; which the FO immediately did. We were then about 350-400 feet and stable; and I continued to a normal landing. After landing I realized; even though there was adequate time to set the flaps to 30; I should have gone around. We debriefed after parking. The FO said he just missed the flaps in setting and checking on the checklist; most likely due to being low time in aircraft and rusty from time off. I missed his wrong setting; due to the dark and the gusty winds requiring my attention. I said I should have gone around; and that he as well should have told me to. I am confident if I had not immediately recognized why we got the too low flaps and immediately corrected it; I would not have continued.

column121: I was the flying pilot; we were kept high the whole time. Then we were given [Runway] XXR GPS Y; as we turned; the turn was inside the approach fix; it was tight.  I turned to final and we looked high and I wasn't comfortable; so we asked for a go-around. I initiated TOGA; we were approximately 2;200 feet when we did the go-around; and began the go-around process and around 2;500 feet. ATC asked to maintain 3;000 feet. We got extremely busy and by the time we realized it we were at 3;500 feet.  We descended to 3;000 feet and we asked to extend the downwind.I had been out for a year and half; and since I gotten back; I did not fly that much; also was off last month and half of this month due to COVID-19; but also I should have asked for extended downwind in the airport; should not [have] allowed the Controller to rush us and I have learned a lesson.Don't accept any arrival that I am not comfortable with.

column121: We were cleared for a visual approach following a Boeing 777 to Runway 25L at LAX. On short final we encountered what was most likely wake turbulence from the proceeding aircraft on short final ~300 feet AGL. This presented as a roll and shear to a headwind. This apparent shear to a headwind along with an addition of some thrust put me high on the profile and ~15 knots above target speed. The severity of the event was below what I would describe as an 'upset'. When the turbulence subsided we appeared to be in a good position to land; so I continued the approach and landed. Being high on profile and bleeding off the excess speed in the flare resulted in a touchdown that was most likely beyond the touchdown zone. Despite this we were in position; with normal braking; to exit at taxiway H8. Tower instructed us to exit at H9.
column123: Upon landing on Runway 25L at LAX we landed outside of the normal 3000 feet of the touchdown zone after being preceded by a 777 to landing during light tail wind conditions. The briefing and approach were completely routine. We had originally planned to land on 24R but we were prepared for a possible change to Runway 25L. We were told on downwind that we would be following a heavy 777 and could expect 25L. Conditions were clear and we had the aircraft in sight. As I reconfigured the aircraft for landing on the south runway; the Captain made the comment that he would be prepared to fly a dot high. The Captain flew as he briefed and we experienced mostly calm winds all the way down the glideslope. By 200 feet AGL; we were back on glide slope.  The aircraft sank somewhat and by 100 feet we were almost a half dot low when we started to feel some of the wake of the 777.  The winds were still light; about 300/7.  The plane was very controllable and the Captain pitched up and added power slightly to stay on top of the wake. In doing so; the speed increased almost 15 knots and I considered calling for a go around.  It still appeared that we were going to be touching down in the first 3000 feet so I allowed the approach to continue.  The Captain flared the aircraft so that it looked like we would touch down right at about 3000 feet.  Without trying to hold the aircraft off; the plane continued down the runway at what seemed like 1 foot. The aircraft finally settled a couple hundred feet later and we turned off the runway and proceeded to the gate without any further issues.This was a case where one limit almost being crossed led to another limit being crossed. It looked like it was all going to work out.  We have all encountered 'disturbed' air from a wake before on landing especially at busy airports like LAX.  The question is how much of an adjustment do you have to make and at what point do you go around.  This looked very doable.  We had plenty of runway and performance.  This looked familiar with a landing in the touchdown zone.  I only thought later and realized that the Captain had elected to do a flaps 40 landing in a 737-900ER.  I know I have not done many flaps 40 landings in a 900ER in the last couple years; so I think my sight picture was off a bit for the flare and the touchdown.  We may have also gotten a bit more of ground effect than what I would have imagined.  The speed did not bleed off as fast as I had anticipated.

column121: Cleared for visual approach to Runway XXL on modified downwind; at 3;000 feet. Maneuvered aircraft to arrive at ZZZZZ at 3;000 to intercept LOC/ Glideslope simultaneously. Thought I was going to be above glideslope at ZZZZZ; asked FO to set 2;000 feet acquired Localizer inside of ZZZZZ; still at flaps 3. Drove into glideslope; selected flaps full. Radio altimeter briefly showed 800 feet due to terrain variation. Broke SOP of being fully configured by 1;000 feet.I accepted a night visual approach after a 12 hour; three leg duty day. Fatigue was a contributor. Hurried visual; should have intercepted final at IAF vs. FAF. My currency was spotty due to reduced flying on reserve.Never accept short visual approaches at end of long duty day.
column123: I was acting as PM. Approaching ZZZ at night from the SW with a high overcast layer above. ATC had us on a downwind leg for [Runway] XXL at 4;000. The autopilot and auto thrust were engaged. Approximately abeam the end of the runway; ATC asked us if we had the field in sight. The CA/PF says he does; so I reported the field in sight. ATC then cleared us for the visual XXL. The PF then set 3;000 in the FCU and I confirmed and referenced the plate showing the FAF ZZZZZ at 3000. I believe the PF went open descent to 3;000 from 4;000. The PF then began his base leg turn. The intercept angle initially looked like it was going to join the final just outside of the FAF. As we approached 3;000 the PF continued the turn in. He asked me to set 2;000 into the FCU and set 1;000 v/s down. I complied as it appeared to me that the PF was trying to intercept the course and I assumed he was going to join inside of ZZZZZ/FAF. He readjusted his intercept to join the final at ZZZZZ or just outside it. By that time I began to realize we were getting low on the profile. I still had visual contact with the runway. At approximately that time; the airplane leveled at 2000 just inside of ZZZZZ and ZZZ tower advised us of a low altitude alert and to check our altitude with the current altimeter setting. I replied with our call sign. We continued at 2;000 until intercepting the glideslope and landed safely. We were approaching the end of a long duty day and it was a non eventful and quiet flight; so fatigue was a possible factor. I am also familiar with operating into ZZZ; so I was a bit complacent; as well as being in VFR conditions and being able to see the runway. Having both pilots confirm and verify any further altitude changes in the FCU after the setting of the lowest charted altitudes for the approach used.

column121: While on a visual approach to [Runway] XXR; I was the PF. I hadn't flown in a month and a half or so; and the weather was conducive to trying to practice skills; so I turned off the AP/AT/FD. While over the ZZZ1 area; we received an RA directing me to level off. After complying with the RA; I began to descend to catch up to the approach; but at the altitude I was at; and the descent rate required to get back on the approach; I would not have been able to do so within stabilized approach criteria; so I conducted a go-around.The go-around was not smooth; and left room for improvement; but all call outs were made; and the checklists were accomplished. After passing through about 1;300 feet or so; we were assigned an altitude of 2;000 feet. The FO selected 2;000 feet and made the comment that we were about 700 feet away. We were climbing at a high rate as we were in TOGA (Still no AP/AT); and I was trying to get the plane slowed down; and arresting the climb rate; the FD began to turn to follow the missed approach for the ILS; as we had loaded it as the backup. We had yet received instruction as to what direction to turn; since we were assigned the visual approach. Between the FD turning and leveling off; it was at this point that I was focused on wanting the aircraft to continue straight on runway heading; and I flew through the 2;000 feet restriction; to my recollection by about 300 feet. The PM attempted to get some sort of instruction from ATC as to what they would like us to do; but struggled to get a word in on the radio. Finally they called and assigned us a vector. They also said since we went past 2;000; to level off at 2;500 feet. At this point; we had a moment; and was able to match up the information on the PFD with what I was wanting; and was able to add back in the AP/AT.This event felt like a culmination of a few problems. The root cause is that I; as the Captain; wasn't as prepared as I should have been. Having flown so little the last few months after upgrade sign off in month; I have been limited to just XX hours of total flying since month; my skill upkeep has been mainly book studying. While this is important; it does not replace being behind the controls of an aircraft.

column121: I was the PF (Pilot Flying) on Aircraft X from ZZZ2-ZZZ1. I flew the RNAV RNP [Runway] XX approach with rain and wind in the vicinity. I attempted to configure early but a strong tailwind made it challenging. I encountered a wind gust which caused me to counter increasing airspeed by pitching up and slightly reduce power to maintain airspeed and continue configuring. This resulted in a position higher than anticipated. The PM (Pilot Monitoring) noticed this also and we articulated that. About that time; ATC directed a go-around. We executed the missed approach and climbed to 3;000 [feet] on vectors for another approach.Noting the conditions; I configured early for the second RNAV RNP XX approach. Rain and wind were still predominant throughout the approach but fewer gust excursions and we remained stable throughout the approach all the way to minimums. At minimums; we both had the approach lights and runway environment in sight and I continued the approach. As I turned final aligning with Runway XX; I lost sight of the runway environment due to the weather. The PM called go around and I initiated go around procedures. We climbed to 3;000 feet on vectors as directed by ATC. We communicated with one another and ensured we were both in the green; we had ample fuel; and agreed to attempt a third approach. On the third RNAV RNP XX approach; I configured early again and remained stable throughout the approach. The wind was still a factor though and required careful monitoring and aircraft correction to maintain a stable approach. Fully configured and in the vicinity of arriving at minimums; ATC advised a wind shift on the field of 330 degrees at 26 knots of wind. ATC asked for our intentions. Due to airline limitations on Maximum Tailwind Component for Landing; the PM told ATC we could not land and we would go-around. ZZZ1 Tower handed us off to ZZZ Departure and they advised us that they 'turned the airfield around' and they would give us vectors for the ILS RWY XY approach and we would be 'number 1' to the field. Again; the Captain and I communicated that we were both in the green; we noted that we had ample fuel; and we prepared for an ILS approach to Runway XY.After intercepting the localizer for the ILS to Runway XY; I configured fully and continued down the glideslope from the final approach fix. It was raining outside and was windy but we remained stable. Shortly prior to 1;000 feet AGL; it grew increasingly difficult to maintain a stable approach. I told the PM that I didn't feel comfortable; the weather was worsening; and it was likely that we would not be stable at 1;000 feet. We agreed and elected to go-around. Just as I began the go around procedure; we encountered a Wind Shear Warning and I proceeded with the Wind Shear escape maneuver. We exited the conditions quickly and transitioned to ATC's directives. We quickly agreed to divert to ZZZ3 and the PM informed ATC of our intention to divert. We climbed to 3;000 feet and flew vectors to ZZZ3.We flew the ILS RWY XYC approach and landed safely at ZZZ3 and taxied to the gate. We shut down the aircraft with approximately 7.4k pounds of fuel remaining. During the deplaning process; the Captain coordinated with the Maintenance and Dispatch and agreed to continue on to ZZZ1 as the weather cleared the area. We added fuel departing with 11.8k pounds of fuel. The Captain flew as the PF and I was the PM for the return flight to ZZZ1. The Captain maintained appropriate communication throughout the event. He communicated with the passengers explaining as best as possible what occurred and what we were doing to ensure their safety while doing our best to land at our intended destination. He kept the flight attendants apprised of the situation after each approach and we discussed each approach; verified we were both in the green; and maintained situational awareness at all times.
column123: Flew RNAV RNP [Runway] XX approach. FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying) and was configuring the aircraft early. He was at gear down; flaps 15; and we encountered a wind gust that drove the airspeed indication almost to redline. The FO raised nose slightly and reduced power to slow the airspeed in order to continue configuring the aircraft. As this occurred; aircraft got a little high. As PM; I noticed this and was in process of informing FO when ATC said that we looked a little high and to just go around. We executed the missed approach as directed by ATC and climbed on a heading to 3;000 ft. to vector for another approach.On the second RNAV RNP XX; FO configured the aircraft early and shot approach to minimums. At minimums; approach lights and runway lights were visible; but as we turned to final approach; visibility was lost.  I called go-around; and FO initiated go-around. We climbed on a heading to 3;000 feet. We communicated with each other that we were both in the green; had plenty of fuel; and prepared for another approach.On the third RNAV RNP XX; the FO had fully configured the aircraft; when ATC advised that the wind had shifted out of the north at 330 degrees and we had 26 knots of wind at our tail. ATC asked our intentions; I told them that we could not land with those winds and that we were going around. At that point; ATC handed us off to departure; and departure immediately advised us that they had turned the airport around and could give us an immediate left downwind to be number 1; shooting ILS Runway XY. FO and I communicated with each other that we were both in the green; had plenty of fuel; and prepared for an approach using the new runway.On attempt to Runway XY; we intercepted localizer and G/S. We had continual high winds indicating to me that the aircraft would not be able to remain stable throughout approach and landing. As PIC; I called for a go-around. FO communicated to me his agreement; and I immediately notified Tower of our intention to divert to ZZZ3. FO initiated the miss. As he did; we encountered a brief Wind Shear Warning. FO finished cleaning aircraft up and we diverted to ZZZ3 without incident.We were vectored to ZZZ3 and landed on Runway XYC and taxied to the gate with 7.4 pounds of fuel. I called Dispatch and she said we had plenty of fuel to get to ZZZ1 but as a precaution due to the frontal weather passage; she added more fuel and brought us up to 11.8 pounds. After the front passage; I flew the aircraft as PF uneventfully to ZZZ1. During all of the approaches and the divert I kept the flight attendants up to date and communicated with the passengers at all times. We exercised good situational awareness and followed SOPs during the entire flight.

column121: I had filed to shoot some approaches at ZZZ; ZZZ1 and return home to ZZZ2. After departing ZZZ2; I was level at 4;000 ft. Upon trimming the plane the Aspen PFD went black in IMC. I was now partial panel with a cloud deck at 1;600 MSL and unknown where the ceilings were. After some time the unit restarted and hung on the ADAHRS initialization screen. Best I can tell from downloading data from the unit; I lost the Aspen for approximately 60 seconds. At the same time; I momentarily lost GPS signal on the Avidyne IFD540. This is an even larger issue as the Aspen E5 uses GPS inputs as part of its ADAHRS calculations. I immediately requested priority handling with Regional Approach. In an unknown amount of time the GPS signal came back. The Aspen now was displaying erroneous wind calculations which led me to have little confidence in the reliability of the unit.It is worth noting that we have had a previous issue with Aspen on inflight resets. The latest unit from Aspen had approximately 25 hours and had not demonstrated any reliability concerns up until this flight. The hardware/software combination that we're running on the Aspen is not covered by AD 2020-16-08.After two vectoring turns and one descent to MVA they cleared me for ILS XX approach into ZZZ2. When cleared for the approach I was at 3;000 ft. instead of the typical 2;000 ft. I was hyper focused on capturing the localizer that I didn't start a descent. In retrospect; from when I was cleared for the approach to when I would have been at Glide Slope intercept; I still would have been way too high. I flew the approach as a localizer; but with a high rate of descent approximately 1;000 FPM. I did not capture the Glide Slope until I was below the cloud deck at approximately 1;000 AGL.My decision to fly a steeper than acceptable approach was due to: Two avionics issues in flight; limited experience post instrument rating on partial panel; task saturation on capturing the localizer. I have been in contact with both manufacturers of equipment and are trying to get to the root cause.
column125: Bonanza 35 pilot reported the PFD going blank inflight in IMC.
